## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Conceptual)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Define Additive Manufacturing. Mention any two advantages of AM over conventional manufacturing.**

**Model Answer (3-Mark structure):**

*Definition (1.5 marks):*
Additive Manufacturing (AM) is a process of joining materials to make objects from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive and formative manufacturing methodologies (ISO/ASTM 52900:2015).

*Any two advantages (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):*
1. **Design Freedom:** Complex internal geometries such as lattices, conformal channels, and topology-optimized structures can be produced without additional tooling.
2. **Material Efficiency:** The buy-to-fly ratio is close to 1:1, generating minimal scrap (typically < 10%) compared to subtractive processes which often waste 70–90% of the raw stock.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**What is an STL file? Why is it needed in Additive Manufacturing?**

**Model Answer (3-Mark structure):**

*Definition (1.5 marks):*
STL (Standard Tessellation Language) is a file format that represents the surface of a 3D CAD model as a collection of triangular facets. Each triangle is defined by the Cartesian coordinates of its three vertices and a unit normal vector (the "stereo-lithography" rule).

*Need in AM (1.5 marks):*
1. STL acts as the **universal data-exchange bridge** between any CAD software and any AM machine. Most slicers and machine controllers accept only STL (or its modern equivalents: **3MF, OBJ, AMF**).
2. It allows the slicer to **approximate the curved CAD surface** with planar triangles, which can then be sliced into horizontal layers of uniform thickness $t$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often write *"STL stands for Stereo Lithography"* — that is the historical origin, but the official modern expansion is **Standard Tessellation Language**. Writing the older form is acceptable but writing the wrong *Y-axis direction* (the "right-hand rule" for normal vectors) in the model answer will cost 0.5 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14-Mark Module Choice Pattern)

> **Note on KTU Pattern:** Module 1 carries 14 marks. You are given an **internal choice** — answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B in full.

---

### **Choice A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **basic principle of Additive Manufacturing**. List the **ISO/ASTM 52900 seven categories** with one example process in each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical part of diameter $D = 50$ mm and height $H = 80$ mm is to be fabricated by FDM with a layer thickness $t = 0.2$ mm. The scanning time per layer is $T_{scan} = 25$ s and the recoating time is $T_{recoat} = 5$ s. The machine setup time is $T_{setup} = 1200$ s. Calculate: **(i)** the number of layers, **(ii)** the total build time in hours, and **(iii)** the theoretical sidewall roughness. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A:**

**(a) Basic Principle + Seven Categories (7 marks):**

**Principle (3 marks):**
The basic principle of AM is the **layer-by-layer addition of material** to build a physical object directly from a 3D digital model. The process follows the **C.S.S.B.P** pipeline:

1. A 3D model is designed in CAD software.
2. The model is converted into an **STL file** (triangulated mesh).
3. The STL is **sliced** into N horizontal layers of uniform thickness $t$.
4. Each layer is fabricated by selectively **fusing/depositing** material using thermal, chemical, or optical energy.
5. The build platform moves down by $t$ and the next layer is added. After N layers, the part is complete and **post-processed** (support removal, surface finishing).

> **Sketch (2 marks):** A schematic showing a CAD model → slicer → layer-by-layer build → finished part (refer to Section 4.1 diagram).

**Seven Categories Table (2 marks):**

| # | Category | Example |
|---|----------|---------|
| 1 | Vat Photopolymerization (VPP) | SLA |
| 2 | Material Extrusion (MEX) | FDM |
| 3 | Material Jetting (MJT) | PolyJet |
| 4 | Binder Jetting (BJT) | ExOne Metal Binder Jetting |
| 5 | Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) | SLM |
| 6 | Sheet Lamination (SHL) | LOM |
| 7 | Directed Energy Deposition (DED) | WAAM |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 50$ mm, $H = 80$ mm, $t = 0.2$ mm, $T_{scan} = 25$ s, $T_{recoat} = 5$ s, $T_{setup} = 1200$ s.

**(i) Number of layers (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \left\lceil \frac{H}{t} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{80}{0.2} \right\rceil = 400 \text{ layers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total build time (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{layer} &= T_{scan} + T_{recoat} = 25 + 5 = 30 \text{ s} \\
T_{build} &= N \times T_{layer} + T_{setup} \\
T_{build} &= 400 \times 30 + 1200 = 12{,}000 + 1{,}200 = 13{,}200 \text{ s} \\
T_{build} &= \frac{13{,}200}{3600} = 3.667 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Theoretical sidewall roughness (2 marks):**

For a vertical cylindrical wall in FDM, the theoretical surface roughness equals approximately half the layer thickness:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{a,\text{ideal}} &\approx \frac{t}{2} = \frac{0.2}{2} = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 100\ \mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Choice B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the difference between **subtractive, formative, and additive manufacturing** with a comparative table. What is the **buy-to-fly ratio**, and why is it important in aerospace? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **working principle of Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** as a representative Powder Bed Fusion process. State any **four process parameters** that control the density of the final part. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B:**

**(a) Subtractive vs. Formative vs. Additive + BTR (7 marks):**

**Comparison (4 marks — 2 for table, 2 for explanation):**

| Aspect | Subtractive | Formative | Additive |
|--------|-------------|-----------|----------|
| Approach | Remove material | Force material into a shape | Add material layer by layer |
| Starting stock | Solid block/billet | Molten/ductile bulk | Powder, wire, filament, sheet |
| Tooling | Cutting tools (milling cutter, insert) | Dies and molds (high cost) | No tooling |
| Wastage | High (chips) | Low (flash) | Very low (~5%) |
| Geometric complexity | Limited by tool access | Limited by mold drafting | Almost unlimited |
| Batch economy | Best for large batches | Best for large batches | Best for low volume + customization |

**Buy-to-Fly Ratio (3 marks):**
The **Buy-to-Fly Ratio (BTR)** is defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
BTR = \frac{\text{Mass of raw material purchased}}{\text{Mass of final usable part}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a milled aerospace bracket, BTR may range from **8:1 to 25:1** (you buy 10 kg of titanium billet to get 1 kg of bracket). In AM, BTR is close to **1.2:1 to 1.5:1** because only the material that becomes the part (plus a small support structure) is used.

**Importance in Aerospace (1 mark):** Aerospace-grade titanium and Inconel alloys cost **$50–$500 per kg**. A BTR reduction from 15:1 to 1.3:1 translates to a **> 90% cost saving on raw material**, plus weight reduction (and hence fuel saving) for the aircraft.

---

**(b) Selective Laser Melting (SLM) Working Principle (7 marks):**

**SLM Working Principle (5 marks):**

SLM is a **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** process for metals. The working principle is as follows:

1. A thin layer of metal powder (e.g., Ti-6Al-4V, AlSi10Mg, 316L SS, IN718) of thickness $t$ (typically **20–80 µm**) is spread across the build platform by a **recoater blade/roller**.
2. A high-power **fiber laser** (typically **200 W – 1 kW**, wavelength ~1070 nm) selectively scans the powder bed according to the sliced 2D toolpath.
3. The laser energy **fully melts** the metal powder (as opposed to SLS, which sinters). A **molten track** forms and fuses metallurgically to the previous solidified layer.
4. After one layer is complete, the build platform descends by $t$, a new layer of powder is spread, and the process repeats.
5. The build chamber is filled with **inert gas (Argon or Nitrogen)** to prevent oxidation of reactive metals like titanium and aluminum.
6. After the build, the part is embedded in unsintered powder; it is removed, depowdered, and then sent for **post-processing** (stress relief, HIP, machining of critical interfaces).

**Four Process Parameters Controlling Final Density (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Laser Power ($P$)** — Higher power increases melt pool depth and reduces lack-of-fusion porosity.
2. **Scan Speed ($v$)** — Lower speed increases energy density; too high → porosity; too low → keyholing.
3. **Layer Thickness ($t$)** — Thinner layers improve resolution and density but slow the build.
4. **Hatch Spacing ($h$)** — Smaller spacing increases overlap between adjacent scan tracks, reducing inter-track porosity.

The combined effect is captured by the **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
VED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \text{(J/mm}^3\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

A typical optimum VED for Ti-6Al-4V is **60–120 J/mm³**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. Writing *"3D Printing"* as the answer to *"Define AM"* — loses 0.5 mark. Use the **formal** term *Additive Manufacturing*.
> 2. Forgetting to convert seconds to hours in numerical problems — loses 0.5 mark.
> 3. Confusing **SLS** (sintering, polymer) with **SLM** (melting, metal) — these are distinct processes.
> 4. Not labeling the **axes of the schematic** (Build Platform, Recoater, Laser, Powder Bed) in part-(a) of a 14-mark question — loses 1 mark.
> 5. Writing the seven categories as *"FDM, SLA, SLS, DLP, PolyJet, Binder Jetting, WAAM"* (process names) instead of as **categories** (VPP, MEX, PBF, MJT, BJT, SHL, DED) — loses up to 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1**

- **Definition:** AM = layer-by-layer material addition per **ISO/ASTM 52900:2015**. (Not 3D Printing alone.)
- **Three Manufacturing Paradigms:** Subtractive (waste), Formative (mold), Additive (free-form).
- **Five-Stage Workflow:** **C**AD → **S**TL → **S**lice → **B**uild → **P**ost-process.
- **STL File:** Triangulated mesh; vertices + normal vectors. Modern alternatives: 3MF, AMF, OBJ.
- **Seven Categories:** VPP, MEX, MJT, BJT, PBF, SHL, DED. (Mnemonic: **"V**ery **M**odern **M**achines **B**uild **P**arts **S**moothly via **D**eposition")
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $N = H / t$
  - $T_{build} = N \cdot (T_{scan} + T_{recoat}) + T_{setup}$
  - $R_a \approx t/2$ for vertical walls
  - $VED = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$
- **Buy-to-Fly Ratio:** ~**1:1 for AM** vs. **8:1 to 25:1** for aerospace subtractive. Economic driver.
- **Layer Thickness Range:** **0.02 mm (SLA) to 0.4 mm (FDM)**. Thinner = smoother but slower.
- **Relative Density Target:** **> 99%** for aerospace/medical SLM parts.
- **First commercial AM patent:** Stereolithography (SLA) by **Chuck Hull, 1984**.
- **Inert Atmosphere:** Required for SLM/DMLS of Ti and Al → Argon or Nitrogen purge.
- **Energy Sources Mapping:** Laser (SLA, SLM, SLS), Electron Beam (EBM), UV Lamp (DLP, MJT), Heat (FDM), Binder (BJT).
- **Staircase Effect:** Curved/inclined surfaces show stair-stepping; roughness scales with $t$ and inversely with build angle $\theta$.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Basic Principle of Additive Manufacturing** ✅

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedure of Product Development in Additive Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT415 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Procedure of Product Development in Additive Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Product Development in Additive Manufacturing (AM)** is defined as the **systematic, sequential engineering workflow** that transforms a market need or design idea into a fully functional physical artefact by integrating **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)**, **digital data preparation (STL/AMF/3MF)**, **process planning**, **layer-by-layer fabrication**, and **post-processing operations** under the constraints of AM process capabilities.

In the context of **PERUT415 (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1)**, the procedure is treated as a **closed-loop, seven-stage generic product development cycle** that is *process-agnostic* — meaning the same fundamental steps apply to Vat Photopolymerisation (VP), Material Extrusion (ME), Powder Bed Fusion (PBF), Binder Jetting (BJ), Material Jetting (MJ), Directed Energy Deposition (DED), and Sheet Lamination (SL).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of AM product development like **building a multi-layered cake from a photograph**:
> 1. You first **decide what cake to bake** (concept).
> 2. You **sketch the cake on paper** (CAD model).
> 3. The sketch is **converted into hundreds of thin horizontal slices** (STL → slicing).
> 4. A **3D printer (the baker)** deposits each slice one on top of the other.
> 5. Once cooled, you **add icing, trim edges, and fix defects** (post-processing).
> 6. Finally, you **taste-test and serve** (testing & validation).

Just as a baker cannot skip the slicing step, an AM engineer cannot skip the **digital slicing step** — it is the *heart* of layer-wise additive fabrication.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Conventions

- **Standard file formats:** **STL** (Stereolithography, de-facto industry standard), **AMF** (Additive Manufacturing File, ISO/ASTM 52915), **3MF** (3D Manufacturing Format, Microsoft-led consortium).
- **Default unit in STL:** **millimetre (mm)** unless otherwise specified in the header.
- **Build envelope:** The maximum printable volume of an AM system, typically given as **L × W × H** in **mm** or **cm**.
- **Layer thickness (h):** Typically between **0.02 mm and 0.4 mm**.
- **Tolerance grade (ISO/ASTM 52900):** Absolute dimensional accuracy, generally **±0.1 mm to ±0.5 mm** for desktop systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Generic Stages of AM Product Development

> [!NOTE]
> According to the **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** standard and the KTU 2024 syllabus, AM product development is a **linear-then-iterative** sequence. Each stage feeds the next, but validation at any stage may trigger a **back-loop** redesign.

**Stage 1 — Concept & Requirements Definition (CRD)**
- Identify the **functional need**, target user, performance criteria (load, temperature, biocompatibility), and **AM-suitability** of the geometry.
- *Why:* A part designed for casting cannot be directly printed — design must be **DFAM-compliant** (Design for Additive Manufacturing).
- *How:* Use Quality Function Deployment (QFD) and Design for X (DFX) tools.

**Stage 2 — Conceptual CAD Modelling**
- Create a **solid or surface model** in CAD software (SolidWorks, Fusion 360, CATIA, Creo).
- *Why:* AM requires a **watertight, manifold 3D solid**; open surfaces or non-manifold edges will fail STL export.
- *How:* Apply DFAM principles — minimise overhangs, avoid trapped volumes, integrate lattice structures for weight reduction.

**Stage 3 — Data Conversion (CAD → STL/AMF/3MF)**
- The tessellated mesh approximates curved surfaces with **triangular facets**.
- *Why:* Native CAD formats (`.sldprt`, `.step`) contain parametric data that AM machines **cannot interpret**; they require triangulated boundary representation.
- *How:* Export with controlled **chordal tolerance** (typically 0.01 mm to 0.05 mm).

**Stage 4 — Slicing & Support Generation**
- Slicer software (Cura, Simplify3D, PrusaSlicer, Magics) cuts the mesh into **2D cross-sections** of thickness equal to the layer height (h).
- *Why:* The AM machine operates in a **2D-plane-per-layer** fashion.
- *How:* Generate **support structures** for overhangs > **45°** from the vertical.

**Stage 5 — Process Planning & Build Setup**
- Select **process parameters**: layer thickness, infill density, scan speed, laser power, build orientation, chamber atmosphere.
- *Why:* Parameters directly affect **density, surface roughness, mechanical strength, and build time**.
- *How:* Nest multiple parts inside the build volume to maximise throughput.

**Stage 6 — Layer-by-Layer Fabrication (Build)**
- The AM machine executes **G-code/M-code** instructions, depositing or solidifying material in successive layers.
- *Why:* This is the **physical materialisation** step — where digital design becomes a tangible object.
- *How:* Monitor the build in real time (in-situ monitoring) for defects.

**Stage 7 — Post-Processing, Testing & Validation**
- Operations: **support removal, depowdering, curing, sanding, polishing, painting, heat treatment, dimensional inspection (CMM), non-destructive testing (CT scan, ultrasonic).**
- *Why:* As-built parts typically do not meet final functional specifications.
- *How:* Compare actual dimensions to CAD nominal using **GD&T** (Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit | Remarks |
|---|---------|----------------------|--------------|---------|
| 1 | STL Triangulation Error | $\epsilon = R(1 - \cos(\theta/2))$ | mm | $R$ = circumscribed radius, $\theta$ = facet angle |
| 2 | File Size Estimate (STL) | $N \approx \dfrac{A}{2 \cdot \epsilon^{2} \cdot \tan(30^{\circ})}$ | triangles | $A$ = surface area |
| 3 | Build Time (Layer-wise) | $T_b = \dfrac{H}{h} \cdot t_{\text{layer}}$ | min | $H$ = part height, $h$ = layer thickness |
| 4 | Volumetric Build Rate | $R_v = \dfrac{V_{\text{part}}}{T_b}$ | $\text{mm}^{3}/\text{s}$ | Key productivity metric |
| 5 | Material Utilisation | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{\text{part}}}{V_{\text{raw}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Higher in extrusion, lower in PBF |
| 6 | Overhang Angle Limit | $\alpha_{\text{crit}} \approx 45^{\circ}$ | degrees | Beyond this, supports are mandatory |
| 7 | Effective Wall Resolution | $r_w = h / \tan(\alpha_{\text{step}})$ | mm | Staircase effect dimension |
| 8 | Surface Roughness (Staircase) | $R_a \approx h \cdot \cos(\theta_{\text{build}})$ | $\mu$m | Minimum at $90^{\circ}$ build angle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are routinely used in **build-time estimation quotations** by service bureaus, **DFAM-driven topology optimisation**, and **process selection** (per Gibson et al., *Additive Manufacturing Technologies*, Springer — a standard reference in KTU 2024-scheme reading lists).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Product Development Workflow

Below is the **complete, traceable procedure** as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus. Every transition is explicitly justified.

**Step 1 — Identify the Product Need**
- Conduct **market research, customer interviews, or problem statements**.
- Define **functional requirements** (e.g., must withstand 50 N load at 80 °C).
- Define **AM-suitability criteria**: complexity, batch size (typically < 1000 units), customisation, weight reduction.

**Step 2 — Conceptual Sketch & DFAM Brainstorming**
- Generate 2D sketches, then convert to 3D primitives.
- Apply **DFAM rules**:
  - Wall thickness ≥ **0.8 mm** (for FDM).
  - Minimum feature size ≥ nozzle/laser spot diameter.
  - Avoid enclosed voids (powder entrapment in PBF).

**Step 3 — Detailed CAD Modelling**
- Use **parametric feature-based modelling** (SolidWorks, NX, Fusion 360).
- Ensure the model is a **single watertight body**.
- Apply **material assignment** in CAD (e.g., PA12, Ti6Al4V, ABS) for thermal/mechanical simulation.

**Step 4 — STL/AMF Export**
- Export the model with the **correct units (mm) and binary STL format** (smaller file size).
- Verify the STL in a mesh-repair tool (Netfabb, Meshmixer) — repair non-manifold edges, inverted normals, and holes.

**Step 5 — Slicing in the Slicer Software**
- Set **layer height h** (e.g., 0.1 mm, 0.2 mm, 0.3 mm).
- Set **infill pattern** (grid, gyroid, honeycomb) and **infill density** (typically 15–50%).
- Set **print speed, nozzle/laser temperature**.
- **Generate supports automatically** for overhangs > 45°.

**Step 6 — Build Orientation & Nesting**
- Orient the part to **minimise supports, reduce staircasing on critical surfaces, and maximise build-volume utilisation**.
- Use the **Z-up convention** unless otherwise required.

**Step 7 — Machine Preparation & Build Execution**
- Load material (filament, resin, powder).
- Calibrate the **build plate** (levelling for FDM; recoater blade for PBF).
- Start the build; monitor via **in-situ sensors** (thermistor, camera, melt-pool monitoring for LPBF).

**Step 8 — Part Removal & Depowdering (if applicable)**
- For FDM: remove from bed using scraper.
- For SLA/DLP: drain uncured resin.
- For PBF: depowder using compressed air or bead-blasting.

**Step 9 — Post-Processing**
- **Support removal** (snipping, wire-EDM, chemical dissolution).
- **Surface finishing** (sanding, bead-blasting, tumbling, polishing, vapour smoothing for ABS in FDM).
- **Heat treatment** (stress-relief annealing for metals, UV post-cure for resins).

**Step 10 — Inspection & Validation**
- **Dimensional inspection** using calipers, CMM, or 3D scanner.
- **Mechanical testing** (tensile, fatigue, hardness) per ASTM/ISO standards.
- **Non-destructive testing** (CT, dye penetrant, ultrasonic).
- Compare against the original **specifications**; iterate if needed.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Stage vs Deliverable vs Tool

| Stage | Deliverable | Tool / Software | KTU Examiner's Focus |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| 1. Need | Requirement Spec | QFD, Kano model | Identifying AM-fitness |
| 2. CAD | 3D model | SolidWorks / Fusion 360 | DFAM rules |
| 3. Conversion | STL/3MF file | CAD export | Triangulation tolerance |
| 4. Slicing | G-code | Cura, Simplify3D | Layer thickness, supports |
| 5. Process Plan | Build sheet | Slicer, machine SW | Orientation, nesting |
| 6. Build | Physical part | AM machine | Parameter effect |
| 7. Post-process | Finished part | Sanding, curing, CT | Inspection methods |
| 8. Validation | Test report | CMM, tensile tester | Specs vs actual |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** Estimate the build time of a cube of side 60 mm printed in FDM with layer height 0.2 mm. Assume average layer print time (including travel) is 12 s/layer. Part height = 60 mm.

**Solution:**

$$
H = 60 \text{ mm}, \quad h = 0.2 \text{ mm}, \quad t_{\text{layer}} = 12 \text{ s/layer}
$$

Number of layers:

$$
n = \dfrac{H}{h} = \dfrac{60}{0.2} = 300 \text{ layers}
$$

Total build time:

$$
T_b = n \times t_{\text{layer}} = 300 \times 12 = 3600 \text{ s} = 60 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Answer:** Approximately **1 hour** for a 60 mm cube at 0.2 mm resolution.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End AM Product Development Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Identify Product Need] --> B[Stage 2: Conceptual Sketch and DFAM]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Detailed CAD Modelling]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Convert CAD to STL AMF 3MF]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Slicing and Support Generation]
    E --> F[Stage 6: Build Orientation and Nesting]
    F --> G[Stage 7: Layer by Layer Fabrication on AM Machine]
    G --> H[Stage 8: Part Removal and Depowdering]
    H --> I[Stage 9: Post Processing Finishing Curing]
    I --> J[Stage 10: Inspection Testing Validation]
    J --> K{Meet Specifications?}
    K -- No --> B
    K -- Yes --> L[Final Approved Product]
```

### 4.2 File Conversion Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Native CAD Format STEP IGES SLDPRT] --> B[STL Triangulated Mesh]
    B --> C[Slicer Software Cura PrusaSlicer]
    C --> D[G-code M-code Machine Instructions]
    D --> E[AM Machine Physical Layer by Layer Build]
```

### 4.3 DFAM Decision Block (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Geometry Input] --> Q{Wall Thickness >= 0.8 mm?}
    Q -- No --> R[Modify CAD Increase Thickness]
    Q -- Yes --> S{Overhang Angle > 45 deg?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Add Support Structures]
    S -- No --> U[Direct Print Possible]
    R --> S
    T --> V[Proceed to Slicing]
    U --> V
```

### 4.4 Process Selection Matrix (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph METAL[Metal AM Family]
        M1[SLM DMLS LPBF]
        M2[EBM Electron Beam Melting]
        M3[DED Directed Energy Deposition]
        M4[BJ Metal Binder Jetting]
    end
    subgraph POLYMER[Polymer AM Family]
        P1[FDM FFF Material Extrusion]
        P2[SLA DLP Vat Photopolymerisation]
        P3[SLS MJF Powder Bed Fusion Polymer]
        P4[MJF MJ Material Jetting]
    end
    subgraph HYBRID[Hybrid and Others]
        H1[Sheet Lamination LOM]
        H2[3D Bioprinting]
    end
    Q[Material Selection] --> METAL
    Q --> POLYMER
    Q --> HYBRID
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*List the standard file formats used in additive manufacturing and state the role of STL.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **STL (Stereolithography)** — de-facto industry standard; tessellates surfaces into triangular facets. (1 mark)
2. **AMF (Additive Manufacturing File)** — ISO/ASTM 52915 standard; supports colour, materials, lattice, and curved triangles. (1 mark)
3. **3MF (3D Manufacturing Format)** — modern, compact, single-archive format from the 3MF Consortium. (1 mark)
*Role of STL:* It acts as the **universal data-exchange bridge** between CAD and AM slicer software, converting parametric solid models into a triangulated mesh that the machine can interpret.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*What is DFAM? Mention any two DFAM guidelines.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**DFAM (Design for Additive Manufacturing)** is a set of design guidelines that exploit the **unique capabilities of AM** (complexity-for-free, part consolidation, lattice structures). (1.5 marks)
Two guidelines: (i) Maintain **minimum wall thickness ≥ 0.8 mm** for FDM to ensure structural integrity; (ii) **Avoid enclosed voids and unsupported overhangs > 45°** to prevent powder entrapment and collapse. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step procedure of product development in additive manufacturing**, with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)
**(b)** Explain the role of **slicing and support generation** in AM. How does the support overhang angle influence the final part quality? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Procedure (7 marks):**
1. **Concept & requirement definition** — Identify need, target specifications, AM-suitability. (1 mark)
2. **Conceptual CAD modelling** — Sketching and 3D modelling using CAD software; apply DFAM rules. (1 mark)
3. **Data conversion to STL/AMF/3MF** — Triangulate the solid model with controlled chordal tolerance. (1 mark)
4. **Slicing** — Convert 3D mesh to 2D layers (h = 0.02–0.4 mm) using slicer software. (1 mark)
5. **Process planning** — Set orientation, infill, supports, machine parameters. (1 mark)
6. **Build execution** — Layer-by-layer fabrication on the AM machine. (1 mark)
7. **Post-processing & validation** — Support removal, finishing, CMM inspection, testing. (1 mark)

*Block diagram:* Refer to the **Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1** of these notes. Examiner expects a labelled block diagram with at least 6 stages. (½ mark reserved for diagram neatness in original answer script.)

**(b) Slicing, Supports & Overhang Angle (7 marks):**
- **Slicing** decomposes the 3D model into a stack of 2D cross-sections, each of which is the toolpath for that layer. (1.5 marks)
- **Support generation** is mandatory for **overhangs > 45°** from the build platform vertical, where self-supporting material cannot be deposited. (1.5 marks)
- Supports prevent **gravity-induced collapse, thermal warping, and residual stress distortion** in PBF and FDM. (1 mark)
- Lower overhang angles (closer to 0° from vertical) **eliminate the need for supports** and reduce post-processing effort. (1 mark)
- However, **horizontal (0° from horizontal)** surfaces exhibit severe **staircase effect**, increasing surface roughness per $R_a \approx h \cdot \cos(\theta_{\text{build}})$. (1 mark)
- The **45° rule** is a heuristic; for high-precision applications, **60–70°** may be needed. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **CAD-to-Part workflow** in additive manufacturing. (7 marks)
**(b)** A part with dimensions 80 mm × 50 mm × 30 mm is to be printed in FDM with a layer thickness of 0.15 mm. If the average layer time is 8 s, calculate the build time and the volumetric build rate. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CAD-to-Part Workflow (7 marks):**
1. **Native CAD model** (SolidWorks, CATIA). (1 mark)
2. **STL export** with chordal tolerance of 0.01–0.05 mm. (1 mark)
3. **STL repair** in Netfabb / Meshmixer — fix non-manifold edges, holes, inverted normals. (1 mark)
4. **Import to slicer** (Cura, Simplify3D) — set layer height, infill, supports. (1 mark)
5. **G-code generation** — layer-by-layer toolpaths. (1 mark)
6. **AM machine build** — physical layer deposition. (1 mark)
7. **Post-processing** — support removal, sanding, inspection. (1 mark)
   *(Examiner tip: A labelled flowchart with arrow directions is worth at least 1 mark.)*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $L = 80$ mm, $W = 50$ mm, $H = 30$ mm, $h = 0.15$ mm, $t_{\text{layer}} = 8$ s.

Part height $H = 30$ mm.

Number of layers:

$$
n = \dfrac{H}{h} = \dfrac{30}{0.15} = 200 \text{ layers}
$$

Build time:

$$
T_b = n \times t_{\text{layer}} = 200 \times 8 = 1600 \text{ s} \approx 26.67 \text{ minutes}
$$

(3 marks for build time)

Part volume:

$$
V = 80 \times 50 \times 30 = 120000 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

Volumetric build rate:

$$
R_v = \dfrac{V}{T_b} = \dfrac{120000}{1600} = 75 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

(3 marks for $R_v$)

**Final Answer:** $T_b = 1600$ s ($\approx 26.67$ min); $R_v = 75$ $\text{mm}^{3}/\text{s}$. (1 mark for correct units and conclusion)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes observed in KTU valuation scripts:**
> 1. **Omitting the block diagram** in part (a) of 7-mark questions — examiners typically reserve **1 mark exclusively for the diagram**. Always draw it.
> 2. **Confusing STL triangulation tolerance with layer thickness** — they are completely independent. STL tolerance affects *file fidelity*; layer thickness affects *build resolution*.
> 3. **Wrong unit in $R_v$** — always express in $\text{mm}^{3}/\text{s}$ (or $\text{cm}^{3}/\text{h}$); writing only "75" without units will cost you at least 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Writing "3D printing" instead of "Additive Manufacturing"** in definition-type questions — KTU 2024 scheme specifically uses the term **Additive Manufacturing**; the older term is acceptable but the modern terminology scores higher.
> 5. **Skipping the validation/back-loop** — the procedure is **iterative**, not strictly linear. Forgetting to mention the iteration from validation back to design will cost you 0.5–1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ☐ AM product development is a **7-to-10 stage, iterative workflow**: Need → CAD → STL → Slicing → Planning → Build → Post-Process → Test.
- ☐ **STL** triangulates; **AMF/3MF** are modern, feature-rich alternatives.
- ☐ **Chordal tolerance** in STL is independent of **layer thickness** in slicing.
- ☐ **DFAM** = Design for AM; minimum wall thickness **≥ 0.8 mm** (FDM), avoid enclosed voids.
- ☐ **Support overhang rule:** angles > **45°** from vertical generally require supports.
- ☐ **Build time formula:** $T_b = (H/h) \times t_{\text{layer}}$.
- ☐ **Volumetric build rate:** $R_v = V_{\text{part}} / T_b$ — a key productivity metric.
- ☐ **Staircase roughness:** $R_a \approx h \cdot \cos(\theta_{\text{build}})$; minimum when part is built **vertically**.
- ☐ **Post-processing** is mandatory for functional parts — include depowdering, support removal, curing, finishing, and **CMM/CT inspection**.
- ☐ **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** is the authoritative standard for AM terminology — memorise the seven process categories.
- ☐ Always include a **neat labelled block diagram** in any 7-mark descriptive question.
- ☐ Numerical problems almost always test **build time** and **volumetric build rate** — practice the formula $T_b = (H/h) \times t_{\text{layer}}$ thoroughly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Additive Manufacturing Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT415 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Additive Manufacturing Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (ASTM F2792 Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Additive Manufacturing (AM)* is defined by **ASTM F2792** as the *"process of joining materials to make objects from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing methodologies."*

The **Classification of Additive Manufacturing Processes** refers to the systematic categorization of these layer-by-layer fabrication techniques based on the **state of the feedstock material**, the **energy source** employed for fusion/joining, and the **mechanism of layer formation**.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus (PERUT415) follows the **ASTM F2792-12** standard, which formally recognizes **seven (7) distinct categories** of AM processes. This is the **board-examination standard** and deviations from this classification scheme typically result in full mark deductions in ESE.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Think of Additive Manufacturing like **building a sandcastle one thin slice at a time**, but instead of wet sand, you use:
- **Liquid resin** hardened by UV light (like freezing ice with a flashlight),
- **Molten plastic** squeezed from a glue-gun (imagine tracing a 3D shape with a hot glue stick),
- **Metal powder** fused by a laser torch (like drawing shapes in sand with a magnifying glass on a sunny day),
- Or even **paper sheets glued and cut** like a stack of sticky notes shaped into a sculpture.

Each method uses a *different material state* and *different energy source* to "glue" or "fuse" the next layer onto the previous one. **The classification of AM processes is essentially a taxonomy of these material-state and energy-source combinations.**

---

### 1.3 Why Classification Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic is High-Yield for KTU:**
> 1. It is a **frequently asked 7–14 mark question** in ESE and internal assessments.
> 2. It forms the **foundational knowledge** for all subsequent modules (Powder Bed Fusion, Material Extrusion, etc.).
> 3. Questions often test **comparative reasoning** (advantages/limitations of each process).

The standardized taxonomy (per ASTM F2792) provides a universal engineering language that allows designers, manufacturers, and researchers to:
- Select the **right process** for a given material and application,
- Predict **mechanical properties**, **tolerance**, and **surface finish**,
- Estimate **cost and lead time** accurately.

---

### 1.4 The Seven ASTM-Recognized AM Process Categories (Master List)

| **#** | **ASTM Category** | **Common Commercial Process Names** | **Feedstock State** |
|:-----:|:------------------|:------------------------------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | **VAT Photopolymerization (VPP)** | Stereolithography (SLA), DLP, LCD | Liquid Photopolymer Resin |
| 2 | **Material Jetting (MJT)** | PolyJet, MultiJet Modeling (MJM), Nano Particle Jetting | Liquid Droplets / Wax |
| 3 | **Binder Jetting (BJT)** | Powder Bed Printing, ColorJet | Powder + Liquid Binder |
| 4 | **Material Extrusion (MEX)** | Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM/FFF), DIW | Thermoplastic Filament / Paste |
| 5 | **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** | Selective Laser Sintering (SLS), Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS), Selective Laser Melting (SLM), Electron Beam Melting (EBM) | Powder (Polymer / Metal) |
| 6 | **Sheet Lamination (SHL)** | Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM), Ultrasonic AM (UAM) | Sheets (Paper / Metal / Polymer) |
| 7 | **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** | Laser Metal Deposition (LMD), Electron Beam AM (EBAM), Wire Arc AM (WAAM) | Powder or Wire (Fed Simultaneously) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners expect the **full ASTM nomenclature** (e.g., *Material Extrusion* — NOT just "FDM"). Memorize the abbreviations: **VPP, MJT, BJT, MEX, PBF, SHL, DED**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Classification

The ASTM F2792 classification is built on **three orthogonal pillars** that define every AM process:

#### Pillar I — State of the Feedstock Material

- **Liquid** (Resins, photopolymers) → VPP, MJT
- **Powder** (Polymers, metals, ceramics) → PBF, BJT
- **Solid Filament/Wire** → MEX, DED
- **Solid Sheet/Foil** → SHL

#### Pillar II — Energy Source for Fusion/Curing

- **UV Light (Photons)** → VPP, MJT
- **Thermal (Heated Nozzle)** → MEX
- **Laser (Coherent Photons)** → PBF, DED
- **Electron Beam (Charged Particles)** → PBF (EBM), DED
- **Liquid Binder (Chemical Adhesive)** → BJT
- **Adhesive / Ultrasonic / Laser Cutting** → SHL
- **Plasma Arc** → DED (WAAM)

#### Pillar III — Layer-Formation Mechanism

- **Curing point scanning** (VPP, MJT)
- **Nozzle deposition rastering** (MEX, DED)
- **Powder bed raking + energy scanning** (PBF)
- **Binder droplet printing on powder** (BJT)
- **Sheet bonding + cutting** (SHL)

---

### 2.2 Detailed Process-by-Process Analysis

#### 2.2.1 VAT Photopolymerization (VPP)

- **Mechanism:** A vat of liquid photopolymer resin is selectively cured by a UV laser (SLA) or projected UV light (DLP) point-by-point or layer-by-layer.
- **Materials:** Acrylic, epoxy-based photopolymers.
- **Strengths:** Excellent surface finish (Ra 0.01–0.05 mm), high accuracy (±0.1%), fine feature resolution.
- **Limitations:** Limited to photopolymers; requires post-curing; expensive resins; UV-sensitive materials degrade over time.

#### 2.2.2 Material Jetting (MJT)

- **Mechanism:** Droplets of build material (photopolymer or wax) are jetted from print heads (similar to inkjet) and immediately cured by UV light.
- **Materials:** Photopolymers, waxes, biological inks.
- **Strengths:** Multi-material capability (varying hardness/color), high resolution (up to 16 μm), smooth surface.
- **Limitations:** Limited material range, expensive, support material required, low mechanical strength compared to PBF/MEX.

#### 2.2.3 Binder Jetting (BJT)

- **Mechanism:** A liquid bonding agent is selectively deposited onto a powder bed (metal, sand, or polymer) using inkjet-style print heads.
- **Materials:** Metal powders, foundry sand, ceramics, gypsum.
- **Strengths:** No support structures required, large build volumes, full-color printing (sandstone), fast for sand molds.
- **Limitations:** Weak "green" parts requiring post-processing (infiltration/sintering), high porosity, lower accuracy.

#### 2.2.4 Material Extrusion (MEX)

- **Mechanism:** A thermoplastic filament is heated past its glass transition temperature in a nozzle and extruded layer-by-layer (FDM), or a metal/paste feedstock is pushed through a nozzle (Bound Metal Deposition).
- **Materials:** ABS, PLA, PETG, Nylon, PEEK, ULTEM, metal-binder pastes.
- **Strengths:** Low cost, desktop-friendly, wide material range, simple to operate.
- **Limitations:** Visible layer lines (poor surface finish), anisotropic mechanical properties, slow for large parts.

#### 2.2.5 Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)

- **Mechanism:** A recoater spreads a thin layer of powder; an energy source (laser for SLS/DMLS/SLM; electron beam for EBM) selectively fuses the powder cross-section.
- **Materials:** Polyamides (Nylon-12), TPU, stainless steel, Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel, AlSi10Mg, CoCr.
- **Strengths:** High mechanical strength, complex internal geometries (lattice structures), no support needed (powder acts as support).
- **Limitations:** Expensive machines, residual stresses in metals (require HIP), post-processing required, safety hazards (metal powders, EBM X-rays).

#### 2.2.6 Sheet Lamination (SHL)

- **Mechanism:** Sheets of material (paper, polymer, metal foil) are bonded together (by adhesive, ultrasonic welding, or brazing) and cut to shape (by laser or knife).
- **Materials:** Paper, PVC, metal foils.
- **Strengths:** Low cost (paper LOM), multi-material capability (UAM), low residual stress.
- **Limitations:** Limited geometric complexity, low resolution, material waste from cut-outs.

#### 2.2.7 Directed Energy Deposition (DED)

- **Mechanism:** Focused thermal energy (laser, plasma, electron beam) melts material as it is deposited from a nozzle (powder-fed or wire-fed). The deposition head is typically mounted on a multi-axis arm (5-axis or robotic).
- **Materials:** Metals (Ti, Inconel, stainless steel, tool steel), some ceramics.
- **Strengths:** High deposition rates, ability to repair/add features to existing parts, multi-axis freedom, large parts.
- **Limitations:** Poor surface finish, lower resolution, expensive, residual stresses, limited geometric complexity (vs. PBF).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Comparative Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The classification is often accompanied by **process selection metrics**. Although AM is not a calculation-heavy subject, KTU examiners may ask for **quantitative comparisons**. Use the following cheat-sheet table.

| **Metric** | **Symbol/Unit** | **Typical Range Across AM Processes** | **Process with Best (Lowest) Value** |
|:-----------|:----------------|:--------------------------------------|:-------------------------------------|
| Layer Thickness | $t_L$ (mm) | 0.01 to 0.5 | VPP / MJT (0.01–0.05) |
| Build Speed | $v_b$ (cm³/h) | 1 to 500 | BJT / DED |
| Dimensional Accuracy | $\pm \epsilon$ (%) | 0.1 to 0.5 | VPP / MJT |
| Surface Roughness | $R_a$ (μm) | 0.05 to 50 | VPP (0.05) — Worst: FDM (~50) |
| Min. Feature Size | $f_{min}$ (mm) | 0.05 to 1.0 | VPP / MJT |
| Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{UTS}$ (MPa) | 30 to 1100 | PBF (Metal: ~1100 for Ti-6Al-4V) |

#### 2.3.1 Build Time Estimation Formula (Conceptual)

The total build time for any AM process can be approximated as:

$$
T_{build} = \frac{V_{part}}{v_{dep} \cdot \eta_{process}}
$$

Where:
- $T_{build}$ = Total build time (hours),
- $V_{part}$ = Volume of the part to be built (cm³),
- $v_{dep}$ = Volumetric deposition rate of the process (cm³/h),
- $\eta_{process}$ = Process efficiency factor (dimensionless, typically **0.3 to 0.8**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This formula is used in *Design for AM (DfAM)* and *process planning* to estimate lead times and quote manufacturing costs. In a KTU exam, a 3-mark question may ask: *"If a part of 50 cm³ is built by FDM (v = 10 cm³/h) and by DED (v = 100 cm³/h), compare build times."*

#### 2.3.2 Surface Roughness Approximation (Layer-Line Model)

For processes with discrete layer deposition (MEX, SHL), surface roughness is approximately:

$$
R_a \approx \frac{t_L}{2} \cdot \tan(\theta_{layer})
$$

Where:
- $t_L$ = Layer thickness,
- $\theta_{layer}$ = Effective layer angle relative to the build direction.

This explains why **thinner layers** yield **better surface finish** in MEX.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification is not academic — it is used in:

- **Aerospace (GE Aviation, Boeing):** PBF (DMLS) for fuel nozzles, brackets; DED for large titanium structural parts.
- **Medical (Stryker, Formlabs):** VPP for surgical guides; PBF (SLM) for orthopedic implants with lattice structures.
- **Automotive (Bugatti, Local Motors):** PBF and MEX for prototyping and end-use parts.
- **Tooling & Casting:** BJT for sand molds and cores (Voxeljet, ExOne).
- **Architecture & Art:** MJT for full-color concept models; SHL for paper-based architectural mockups.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Build Time Comparison Between Two AM Processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation question**. We will solve it **exhaustively** with no skipped steps.

**Problem:** A hollow cube of side 60 mm with wall thickness 5 mm is to be fabricated.
- **Process A:** Material Extrusion (FDM) with deposition rate $v_A = 12$ cm³/h and efficiency $\eta_A = 0.6$.
- **Process B:** Powder Bed Fusion (DMLS) with deposition rate $v_B = 4$ cm³/h and efficiency $\eta_B = 0.8$.

Calculate the build time for both processes and determine which is faster.

---

#### Step 1: Compute the Volume of the Part

The part is a hollow cube (a cube with a smaller cube removed from the inside).

The outer cube has side $a_{out} = 60$ mm = 6 cm.
The inner cube has side $a_{in} = a_{out} - 2 \cdot t_w$, where $t_w$ = wall thickness = 5 mm = 0.5 cm.

$$
a_{in} = 6 \text{ cm} - 2 \cdot (0.5 \text{ cm})
$$

$$
a_{in} = 6 \text{ cm} - 1 \text{ cm} = 5 \text{ cm}
$$

The volume of material in the hollow cube is:

$$
V_{part} = a_{out}^{3} - a_{in}^{3}
$$

$$
V_{part} = (6)^{3} - (5)^{3}
$$

$$
V_{part} = 216 \text{ cm}^{3} - 125 \text{ cm}^{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{part} = 91 \text{ cm}^{3}}
$$

> **Textual Logic:** A solid 6 cm cube has 216 cm³ of volume. Removing a 5 cm cube from the inside leaves 91 cm³ of actual material to be deposited.

---

#### Step 2: Compute Build Time for Process A (FDM)

Using the formula:

$$
T_A = \frac{V_{part}}{v_A \cdot \eta_A}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
T_A = \frac{91 \text{ cm}^{3}}{12 \text{ cm}^{3}\text{/h} \cdot 0.6}
$$

$$
T_A = \frac{91}{7.2} \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_A \approx 12.64 \text{ hours}}
$$

> **Textual Logic:** FDM has a high nominal rate (12 cm³/h), but only 60% of the motion is productive (efficiency factor), reducing effective throughput to 7.2 cm³/h.

---

#### Step 3: Compute Build Time for Process B (DMLS)

Using the same formula:

$$
T_B = \frac{V_{part}}{v_B \cdot \eta_B}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
T_B = \frac{91 \text{ cm}^{3}}{4 \text{ cm}^{3}\text{/h} \cdot 0.8}
$$

$$
T_B = \frac{91}{3.2} \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_B \approx 28.44 \text{ hours}}
$$

> **Textual Logic:** DMLS has a lower nominal deposition rate (4 cm³/h) because the laser must scan and fuse each cross-section precisely, but its higher efficiency (0.8) reflects less wasted motion (no toolpath re-traversing).

---

#### Step 4: Comparative Conclusion

$$
T_A < T_B \quad \Rightarrow \quad 12.64 \text{ h} < 28.44 \text{ h}
$$

> **Conclusion:** **Material Extrusion (FDM) is approximately 2.25× faster** than Powder Bed Fusion (DMLS) for this part. However, the **mechanical strength** of the DMLS part would be far superior. This is the **classic speed-vs-strength trade-off** in AM process selection.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Process Selection by Application Domain

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU analytical tool**. Memorize the dominant process for each application.

| **Application Domain** | **Primary Process** | **Secondary Process** | **Reason for Selection** |
|:-----------------------|:--------------------|:----------------------|:--------------------------|
| **Investment Casting Patterns** | SHL (LOM with paper) | MJT (wax) | Easy burnout, low cost |
| **Aerospace Brackets (Ti)** | PBF (DMLS/SLM) | DED | High strength-to-weight, lattice structures |
| **Dental Crowns & Bridges** | PBF (DMLS, CoCr) | VPP (SLA for patterns) | Biocompatibility, accuracy |
| **Concept Prototypes (Office)** | MEX (FDM) | VPP (SLA) | Low cost, fast iteration |
| **Full-Color Architectural Models** | MJT (PolyJet) | BJT (ColorJet) | Color realism, multi-material |
| **Sand Molds & Cores** | BJT | SHL | High temperature resistance |
| **Surgical Guides (Patient-Specific)** | VPP (SLA/DLP) | MJT | Sterilizable biocompatible resin, high accuracy |
| **Large Marine Propellers (Metal)** | DED (WAAM) | PBF | High deposition rate, large volume |
| **Hearing Aid Shells** | MJT | VPP | Custom fit, multi-color, biocompatible |
| **Polymer End-Use Parts (Low Volume)** | PBF (SLS, Nylon) | MEX | Isotropic strength, no support |

### 3.3 Hierarchical Classification Tree (Logical Derivation)

The seven AM processes can be derived from a **decision tree** based on feedstock state:

1. **If feedstock is LIQUID**:
   - Cured by light → **VPP** (single material, vat-based) OR **MJT** (jet droplets).
2. **If feedstock is POWDER**:
   - Fused by energy → **PBF** (entire bed in chamber).
   - Bonded by adhesive → **BJT** (binder drops, no fusion).
3. **If feedstock is SOLID FILAMENT**:
   - Melted through heated nozzle → **MEX**.
4. **If feedstock is SOLID SHEET**:
   - Bonded and cut → **SHL**.
5. **If feedstock is POWDER or WIRE, deposited coaxially with energy**:
   - Energy source is laser/electron/plasma → **DED**.

> **Key Insight:** PBF and DED both use powder and energy, but **PBF uses a powder bed and gravity-fed energy**, while **DED uses coaxial powder/wire injection and a moving deposition head** (5-axis freedom). This is a **favourite KTU 7-mark question**.
