   - Shape Memory Polymer 4D Printing
   - Hydro-Responsive Bilayer Printing
7. **Bio-Fabrication Formats**
   - Freeform Reversible Embedding (FRESH)
   - Suspended Bath Bioprinting

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Format (Operational Breakdown)

#### A. Computed Axial Lithography (CAL) — *Volumetric*
- **Step 1:** A photosensitive resin is placed inside a rotating cylindrical cuvette.
- **Step 2:** A **2D light pattern** is projected from a DLP module synchronized with cuvette rotation.
- **Step 3:** Photopolymerization dose accumulates at voxels where the **cumulative light dose** exceeds the gelation threshold $E_c$.
- **Step 4:** The system solves an **inverse problem** using the **Radon Transform** to back-project the desired 3D geometry.
- **Why?** It enables **sub-minute printing of centimetre-scale objects without support structures**.

#### B. Continuous Liquid Interface Production (CLIP) — *Continuous Interfacial*
- **Step 1:** A **dead zone** (~tens of µm) of uncured resin is maintained just above an oxygen-permeable window.
- **Step 2:** UV light cures the resin above the dead zone, while **oxygen inhibition** prevents adhesion to the window.
- **Step 3:** The build platform pulls the part **continuously** (Z-axis) rather than in discrete steps.
- **Why?** It removes the **peel-and-step time**, enabling speeds of **~100 mm/hour** (vs. ~10 mm/hour for conventional SLA).

#### C. Two-Photon Polymerization (2PP) — *Sub-Wavelength*
- **Step 1:** A **femtosecond near-infrared (NIR) laser** (~780 nm) is focused into the resin via a high-NA objective.
- **Step 2:** Photons are absorbed **simultaneously** by a single photoinitiator molecule, providing energy equivalent to a **UV photon (~390 nm)**.
- **Step 3:** Polymerization occurs **only at the focal voxel** (due to the quadratic intensity dependence).
- **Why?** It enables **feature sizes <100 nm**, beyond the optical diffraction limit.

#### D. Melt Electrowriting (MEW) — *Electrohydrodynamic*
- **Step 1:** A polymer melt is extruded from a spinneret under **pneumatic pressure**.
- **Step 2:** A **high voltage (1.5–7 kV)** is applied between the spinneret and a grounded collector.
- **Step 3:** The **Taylor cone** ejects a fine jet (~1–30 µm diameter) that is deposited in a controlled pattern.
- **Why?** It bridges the **resolution gap** between FDM (100s of µm) and 2PP (sub-µm).

#### E. FRESH (Freeform Reversible Embedding of Suspended Hydrogels) — *Suspension-Based Bio*
- **Step 1:** A **thermally-reversible support bath** (gelatin microparticles) holds the print in 3D space.
- **Step 2:** Bio-ink is extruded into the bath, where it is **mechanically supported** from all sides.
- **Step 3:** After printing, the bath is **melted away** at body temperature (~37 °C).
- **Why?** It enables **complex biological scaffolds** without the need for sacrificial support polymers.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all **exam-critical equations** for newly proposed formats. For board exam preparation, memorise the **governing physics equation** and **typical magnitudes**.

| Format | Governing Equation | Variable Definitions | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CAL (Beer–Lambert dose)** | $D(x,y,z) = \sum_{\theta} \int_{0}^{T} I_\theta(x,y,z,t) \cdot e^{-\alpha L}\, dt$ | $I_\theta$ = intensity at angle $\theta$, $\alpha$ = resin attenuation, $L$ = path length | $D \geq E_c$ for cure |
| **CLIP (Dead Zone Height)** | $h_{dz} = \frac{D_{O_2}}{\kappa \cdot C_{O_2}^{surf}}$ | $D_{O_2}$ = O₂ diffusivity, $\kappa$ = O₂ inhibition rate, $C^{surf}$ = surface O₂ conc. | 10–30 µm |
| **2PP (2-Photon Absorption Rate)** | $R_{2PA} = \frac{\delta_{2PA} \cdot I^2}{2 \hbar \omega}$ | $\delta_{2PA}$ = 2PA cross-section, $I$ = laser intensity, $\hbar\omega$ = photon energy | $\delta \sim 10^{-58}$ m⁴s |
| **2PP (Voxel Size)** | $d_v = \frac{0.7 \lambda}{\sqrt{2} \cdot NA}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength, $NA$ = numerical aperture | 50–200 nm |
| **MEW (Taylor Cone Voltage)** | $V_c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma d}{\varepsilon_0}}$ (simplified) | $\gamma$ = surface tension, $d$ = nozzle diameter, $\varepsilon_0$ = permittivity | 1.5–7 kV |
| **CSAM (Critical Velocity)** | $v_{crit} = \sqrt{\frac{F_{adh} \cdot A}{\rho_p \cdot m_p}}$ | $F_{adh}$ = adhesion force, $A$ = contact area, $\rho_p$ = particle density, $m_p$ = particle mass | 300–1200 m/s |
| **UAM (Weld Energy)** | $E_w = \sigma_y \cdot \dot{\varepsilon} \cdot V_{weld} \cdot t$ | $\sigma_y$ = yield stress, $\dot{\varepsilon}$ = strain rate, $V_{weld}$ = weld volume, $t$ = time | Joule |
| **4D (Shape Recovery Stress)** | $\sigma_{rec} = E(T) \cdot (\varepsilon_{prog} - \alpha \Delta T)$ | $E(T)$ = temp-dependent modulus, $\varepsilon_{prog}$ = programmed strain, $\alpha$ = CTE | MPa |
| **FRESH (Yield Stress of Bath)** | $\tau_y = \tau_{gel} \cdot \phi_{gel} \cdot C_{ca}$ | $\tau_{gel}$ = base gel yield, $\phi_{gel}$ = gel fraction, $C_{ca}$ = crosslink activation | 50–500 Pa |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **CAL** → Rapid prototyping of patient-specific implants, lattice structures.
- **CLIP** → Mass production of lattice shoe midsoles (Adidas 4DFWD), automotive elastomer parts.
- **2PP** → Micro-optics (metalenses), photonic crystals, microneedle arrays.
- **MEW** → Vascular tissue engineering scaffolds with micron-scale fibres.
- **UAM** → Multi-material electronics (Cu–Al) with embedded sensors.
- **FRESH** → Full-scale human heart models (e.g., BioLife4D, Carnegie Mellon FRESH printing).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The CAL Inverse Problem (Radon-Transform Reconstruction)

The CAL process is mathematically a **tomographic synthesis problem**. Given a 3D target geometry $f(x,y,z)$, the system must compute the 2D projections $P_\theta(u,v)$ such that the **cumulative dose** in the resin matches the **target geometry's threshold function**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting Premise:** The photopolymer is cured (solidified) only where the cumulative light dose $D(x,y,z) \geq E_c$ (the critical gelation dose). We define the target geometry as a binary function $\Omega(x,y,z) \in \{0, 1\}$ where 1 = solid, 0 = void.

**Step 1 — Forward Problem (Single Projection)**
The dose deposited at a point $(x,y,z)$ by a single 2D projection at angle $\theta$ is given by the **Beer–Lambert attenuated intensity** integrated over exposure time $T$:

$$
D_\theta(x,y,z) = \int_{0}^{T} I_\theta(x,y,z,t) \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot L_\theta(x,y,z)} \, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Cumulative Dose Across All Projections**
As the cuvette rotates, multiple angular projections $\theta \in \{0, \Delta\theta, 2\Delta\theta, \dots, 360^\circ - \Delta\theta\}$ are superimposed. The total dose becomes:

$$
D_{total}(x,y,z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \int_{0}^{T_k} I_k(x,y,z,t) \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot L_k(x,y,z)} \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Curing Condition**
The polymer solidifies only where:

$$
D_{total}(x,y,z) \geq E_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Omega(x,y,z) = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Inverse Problem (Designing the Projections)**
To determine the 2D image at each angle, we use the **Radon Transform** of the target:

$$
P_\theta(u) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \Omega(u \cos\theta - v \sin\theta, \, u \sin\theta + v \cos\theta, z) \, dv
$$

**Step 5 — Dose-Matched Projection Normalisation**
The image to display is then:

$$
I_\theta(u,v) = K \cdot \left[ P_\theta(u) \cdot e^{\alpha L_{max}} - D_{bg} \right]
$$

where $K$ is a scaling constant and $D_{bg}$ is the background dose threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The CAL inverse problem reduces to computing a finite number of **2D Radon projections** of the 3D geometry, normalising for optical attenuation. This is the **same mathematics as a CT scanner, but inverted** — instead of *reconstructing* a 3D object from 2D X-ray images, we *generate* 2D X-ray-like images to *build* a 3D object.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — CLIP Dead-Zone Steady-State Equation

In CLIP, the build window is permeable to **oxygen**, which inhibits free-radical polymerisation. A **steady-state diffusion-reaction balance** determines the dead-zone thickness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting Premise:** Oxygen diffuses upward from the oxygen-permeable membrane; UV light generates radicals that consume O₂. At steady state, the **Fickian diffusion flux equals the radical scavenging rate**.

**Step 1 — Mass Balance on Oxygen**
At steady state:

$$
D_{O_2} \frac{d^2 C_{O_2}(z)}{dz^2} = -R_{scav}(z)
$$

**Step 2 — Radical Scavenging Kinetics**
The O₂ consumption rate is proportional to the **photoinitiator radical generation rate** $R_i$:

$$
R_{scav}(z) = \kappa \cdot R_i \cdot C_{O_2}(z)
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting and Solving**
Substituting into the diffusion equation:

$$
D_{O_2} \frac{d^2 C}{dz^2} + \kappa R_i C = 0
$$

This is a second-order linear ODE with exponential solutions. The general solution is:

$$
C(z) = A \cos(\beta z) + B \sin(\beta z), \quad \text{where} \quad \beta = \sqrt{\frac{\kappa R_i}{D_{O_2}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Boundary Conditions**
- At $z = 0$ (window surface): $C(0) = C_{O_2}^{surf}$ (high O₂ from permeable membrane)
- At $z \to \infty$: $C(\infty) = 0$ (depleted by reaction)

**Step 5 — Critical Dead-Zone Height**
The dead zone is the region where $C(z) \geq C_{crit}$ (the O₂ concentration needed to inhibit polymerisation). Solving yields:

$$
h_{dz} = \frac{1}{\beta} \cdot \arccos\left(\frac{C_{crit}}{C_{surf}}\right)
$$

Expanding with $\beta = \sqrt{\kappa R_i / D_{O_2}}$:

$$
\boxed{h_{dz} = \sqrt{\frac{D_{O_2}}{\kappa R_i}} \cdot \arccos\left(\frac{C_{crit}}{C_{surf}}\right)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The dead zone **grows** when oxygen diffusivity is high or when the radical generation rate is low. This is why CLIP uses a **high-intensity UV lamp** (to increase $R_i$) and a **tuned oxygen-permeable fluoropolymer membrane** (e.g., Teflon AF-2400) to achieve stable dead zones of 10–30 µm.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Two-Photon Polymerization (2PP) Voxel Resolution

In 2PP, the polymerisation rate $R_{2PA}$ has a **quadratic dependence** on the laser intensity $I$, which is why the resolution can beat the diffraction limit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting Premise:** The two-photon absorption rate per photoinitiator molecule is given by the **nonlinear Beer–Lambert law** for quadratic processes.

**Step 1 — Intensity Distribution of Focused Laser**
For a Gaussian beam focused by an objective of numerical aperture $NA$:

$$
I(r,z) = I_0 \left(\frac{w_0}{w(z)}\right)^2 \exp\left(-\frac{2r^2}{w(z)^2}\right)
$$

where $w(z) = w_0 \sqrt{1 + (z/z_R)^2}$ is the beam waist at depth $z$.

**Step 2 — Two-Photon Absorption Rate**
The 2PA rate is:

$$
R_{2PA}(r,z) = \frac{\delta_{2PA} \cdot C_{PI} \cdot I(r,z)^2}{2 \hbar \omega}
$$

where $C_{PI}$ is the photoinitiator concentration.

**Step 3 — Effective Voxel Diameter**
The polymerisation threshold $R_{th}$ defines the boundary of the cured voxel. Setting $R_{2PA}(r = d_v/2, z = 0) = R_{th}$:

$$
\frac{\delta_{2PA} \cdot C_{PI} \cdot I_0^2}{2 \hbar \omega} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{d_v^2}{w_0^2}\right) = R_{th}
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for the Voxel Diameter**

$$
d_v = w_0 \sqrt{\ln\left(\frac{\delta_{2PA} C_{PI} I_0^2}{2 \hbar \omega R_{th}}\right)}
$$

**Step 5 — Expressing in Terms of NA**
Using the Gaussian beam waist relation $w_0 = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{NA}$ (Airy disk approximation):

$$
\boxed{d_v \approx \frac{0.7 \lambda}{NA} \cdot \sqrt{\ln\left(\frac{P_{avg}}{P_{th}}\right)}}
$$

where $P_{avg}$ is the average laser power and $P_{th}$ is the polymerisation threshold power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The voxel size in 2PP is **logarithmically dependent** on the laser power, and **linearly dependent on $\lambda / NA$**. By using a high-NA objective (~1.4) and a 780 nm laser, voxels as small as **~80 nm** are achievable — far below the diffraction limit of single-photon absorption at 390 nm.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Format | Resolution | Speed | Material | Maturity | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CAL | ~50 µm | 5–120 s | Photopolymers | Lab–Pilot | **High** |
| CLIP | ~50 µm | ~100 mm/hr | Photopolymers | Commercial | **High** |
| 2PP | ~100 nm | 10⁶ voxels/s | Photopolymers | Commercial | **Medium** |
| MEW | ~5–30 µm | ~5 mm/s | PCL, PLA | Lab–Pilot | **Medium** |
| UAM | ~50 µm | ~10 cm³/hr | Metals | Commercial | **High** |
| CSAM | ~30 µm | 5–10 kg/hr | Metals | Commercial | **Medium** |
| FRESH | ~200 µm | ~10 mm/s | Hydrogels, Bio-inks | Lab–Pilot | **High** |
| 4D Printing | ~100 µm | mm/s | SMP, Hydrogels | Lab | **Emerging** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classification Architecture of Newly Proposed Formats

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Newly Proposed AM Formats] --> B[Volumetric Tomographic]
    A --> C[Continuous Interfacial]
    A --> D[Sub-Wavelength High-Res]
    A --> E[Field-Assisted]
    A --> F[Reactive Solid-State]
    A --> G[Stimuli Responsive 4D]
    A --> H[Bio-Fabrication]

    B --> B1[Computed Axial Lithography]
    B --> B2[Xolography]
    C --> C1[CLIP]
    C --> C2[HARP]
    D --> D1[2PP]
    D --> D2[PmicroSL]
    E --> E1[Melt Electrowriting]
    E --> E2[Magnetic Field Deposition]
    F --> F1[UAM]
    F --> F2[Cold Spray AM]
    G --> G1[SMP based 4D]
    G --> G2[Hydrogel Bilayer 4D]
    H --> H1[FRESH]
    H --> H2[Suspended Bath]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — CAL Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA[Step 1: Load 3D STL Geometry] --> stepB[Step 2: Compute Radon Projections]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Normalize for Beer-Lambert Attenuation]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Sequentially Project 2D Patterns]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Rotate Cuvette Continuously]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Accumulate Dose in Resin]
    stepF --> stepG{Dose greater than Ec?}
    stepG -->|Yes| cureA[Voxel Solidifies]
    stepG -->|No| cureB[Voxel Remains Liquid]
    cureA --> stepH[Step 7: Drain Uncured Resin]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 8: Post Cure UV Flood]
    stepI --> endNode[Final 3D Part Ready]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — CLIP Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    p1[Step 1: Fill Resin Vat with Photopolymer] --> p2[Step 2: Oxygen Permeable Membrane at Base]
    p2 --> p3[Step 3: Project UV Image from Below]
    p3 --> p4[Step 4: Oxygen Inhibits Cure at Window]
    p4 --> p5[Step 5: Dead Zone Forms 10 to 30 micrometer]
    p5 --> p6[Step 6: Cure Layer Above Dead Zone]
    p6 --> p7[Step 7: Pull Build Platform Up Continuously]
    p7 --> p8{Continuous Z Motion}
    p8 -->|Yes| p3
    p8 -->|No| p9[Step 8: Part Removed and Post Cured]
    p9 --> pEnd[Final 3D Part Ready]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — 2PP Optical Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Step 1: Femtosecond NIR Laser 780 nm] --> M1[Step 2: Beam Expander]
    M1 --> M2[Step 3: Galvanometer Mirrors XY Scan]
    M2 --> M3[Step 4: High NA Objective NA 1.4]
    M3 --> R1[Step 5: Focused into Photoresin]
    R1 --> V1[Step 6: Voxel Cures via 2PA]
    V1 --> PZT[Step 7: Piezo Z Stage for Layer Movement]
    PZT --> R1
    R1 --> M3
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mp[Step 1: Molten Polymer in Syringe] --> pn[Step 2: Pneumatic Pressure Applied]
    pn --> sp[Step 3: Polymer Reaches Nozzle Tip]
    sp --> hv[Step 4: High Voltage 1.5 to 7 kV Applied]
    hv --> tc[Step 5: Taylor Cone Forms]
    tc --> ej[Step 6: Fine Jet Ejected 1 to 30 micrometer]
    ej --> gc[Step 7: Grounded Collector Substrate]
    gc --> xt[Step 8: XY Stage Traverses Pattern]
    xt --> gc
    gc --> fp[Step 9: Fibre Scaffolds Deposited]
```
