## 1.4 Position in the ASTM/ISO Additive Manufacturing Taxonomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021**, LENS falls under the category:
> **Material Extrusion / Directed Energy Deposition → DED-LB (Directed Energy Deposition – Laser Beam)** with **powder feedstock** (denoted *DED-LB-p* in some extended classifications).

It is explicitly **distinct from Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** because:
- In PBF the powder is pre-spread across an entire bed.
- In LENS the powder is **injected only at the point of laser focus**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The LENS process follows a strict sequence of physical events. Each step below is a **high-yield KTU answer point**.

1. **CAD Model Preparation:** A 3D CAD model of the desired part is sliced into thin horizontal cross-sections of thickness *t* (typically **0.2 – 0.5 mm**).
2. **Path Planning:** Toolpath software generates a raster or contour scan pattern with hatch spacing *h* (typically **0.3 – 0.8 mm**).
3. **Substrate Loading:** A metallic substrate (e.g., Ti-6Al-4V plate, SS-304 plate) is placed on a numerically controlled *X-Y-Z* stage inside a sealed **glove chamber**.
4. **Chamber Purge:** High-purity **argon** (or nitrogen) is flushed to bring residual oxygen below **50 ppm**, preventing oxidation of reactive alloys (Ti, Al).
5. **Laser Focusing:** The Nd:YAG / fiber laser beam is delivered through an optical fiber and focused via a **ZnSe lens** to a spot diameter of **0.4 – 2.0 mm** at the substrate.
6. **Powder Injection:** Metal powder is metered from a hopper by a **volumetric or gravimetric feeder** and carried by inert carrier gas (Ar) through **3 or 4 coaxial nozzles** that converge on the laser spot.
7. **Melt Pool Formation:** The laser creates a small, moving **melt pool** typically **0.5 – 2.0 mm wide** and **0.2 – 0.5 mm deep** on the substrate.
8. **Solidification:** As the beam moves away, the molten metal solidifies rapidly (cooling rate **10³ – 10⁵ K/s**), forming a dense, metallurgically bonded track.
9. **Layer Stacking:** The Z-axis is raised by one layer thickness, and the next layer is deposited on top, with **re-melting of ~30 %** of the previous layer ensuring inter-layer bonding.
10. **Closed-Loop Control (Optional):** A **pyrometer or CCD camera** monitors melt-pool temperature/size; feedback adjusts laser power *P* or scan speed *v* in real time.
11. **Cooling & Post-Processing:** The part cools under inert gas, then undergoes optional **HIP (Hot Isostatic Pressing)** or **CNC machining** for final tolerances.

## 2.2 System Architecture

A complete LENS workstation has **five sub-systems**:

| Sub-System | Function | Key Components |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Laser Delivery** | Provides melting energy | Nd:YAG / Fiber laser, optical fiber, focusing head |
| **Powder Delivery** | Metered powder injection | Powder hopper, carrier gas line, mass-flow controller, coaxial nozzles |
| **Motion Control** | Part / beam positioning | 4/5-axis CNC gantry or KUKA / ABB robotic arm |
| **Atmosphere Control** | Oxygen-free environment | Glove box, O₂ analyzer, argon supply |
| **Process Monitoring** | Closed-loop quality control | Melt-pool pyrometer, IR camera, software (e.g., Optomec’s *intellipowder*™) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **high-yield** for KTU Part-B numericals and derivations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** | $E_v \;=\; \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | J/mm³ | *P* = laser power (W), *v* = scan speed (mm/s), *h* = hatch spacing (mm), *t* = layer thickness (mm) |
| 2 | **Linear Energy Density (LED)** | $E_L \;=\; \dfrac{P}{v}$ | J/mm | Energy per unit length of scan |
| 3 | **Specific Energy (Ashby & Easterling)** | $E_s \;=\; \dfrac{P}{v \cdot d}$ | J/mm² | *d* = beam spot diameter |
| 4 | **Aspect Ratio of Melt Pool** | $AR \;=\; \dfrac{w}{d_m}$ | dimensionless | *w* = melt-pool width, *d_m* = melt-pool depth |
| 5 | **Deposition Rate (mass)** | $\dot{m} \;=\; \eta \cdot \dot{m}_{feed}$ | g/s | *η* = powder catchment efficiency (**30 – 70 %**) |
| 6 | **Track Width (empirical)** | $w \;\approx\; d_{spot} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, P}{\pi \, k \, (T_m - T_0)}}$ | mm | *k* = thermal conductivity, *T_m* = melting point |
| 7 | **Powder Catchment Efficiency** | $\eta \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_{deposited}}{\dot{m}_{injected}}$ | % | Function of nozzle angle, gas flow, stand-off distance |
| 8 | **Solidification Cooling Rate** | $\dot{T} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \, k \, (T_m - T_0)^2}{\pi \, \rho \, C_p \, v \cdot d^2}$ | K/s | Rosenthal-type approximation |
| 9 | **Reynolds Number of Powder Stream** | $Re \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_g \, v_g \, D_{nozzle}}{\mu_g}$ | dimensionless | For carrier-gas flow design |
| 10 | **Layer Thickness Relation** | $t \;\leq\; \dfrac{d_m}{2}$ | mm | Geometric safety to avoid lack-of-fusion |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer sheets, **always** state the assumption list (steady-state, 2-D heat flow, constant material properties) before plugging values into Rosenthal-type formulas.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Repair of turbine blades (Inconel 718, Ti-6Al-4V) without scrapping the component.
- **Biomedical:** Custom patient-specific titanium craniofacial implants with graded porosity.
- **Tooling:** Adding wear-resistant Stellite coatings onto die-steel punches.
- **Defense:** On-site repair of marine propeller blades and gun barrels.
- **Energy:** Manufacturing functionally graded transition joints between stainless steel and Inconel for nuclear piping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Laser Power, Scan Speed, and Melt-Pool Width

We consider a **2-D quasi-steady moving heat source** (Goldak’s bi-ellipsoid simplified to a line source for thin deposits). The heat input rate per unit length is:

$$
q \;=\; \dfrac{P}{v} \quad \text{[W/m]}
$$

**Step 1 — Heat Balance at the Melt Pool Boundary**

The heat conducted away from the source into the solid substrate must equal the input minus the latent heat used for melting. For a semi-infinite plate under a moving line source of strength $q$ moving at speed $v$, Rosenthal's 2-D solution gives the **isotherm width** $w$ of any temperature contour $T$:

$$
w(T) \;=\; 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, k \, (T - T_0)}{q / (\pi \, \rho \, C_p \, v)}} \quad \text{(1)}
$$

where $k$ is thermal conductivity, $\rho$ is density, $C_p$ is specific heat, and $T_0$ is the initial substrate temperature.

**Step 2 — Evaluate at the Melting Isotherm $T = T_m$**

Setting the isotherm temperature to the alloy's melting point $T_m$ yields the melt-pool width:

$$
w \;=\; 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, k \, (T_m - T_0)}{\dfrac{P}{\pi \, \rho \, C_p \, v^2}}} \quad \text{(2)}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the Algebra**

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\pi \, \rho \, C_p \, v^2$:

$$
w \;=\; 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \pi \, k \, \rho \, C_p \, (T_m - T_0) \, v^2}{P}} \quad \text{(3)}
$$

Extracting $v$ from the square root:

$$
w \;=\; 2 v \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \pi \, k \, \rho \, C_p \, (T_m - T_0)}{P}} \quad \text{(4)}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce a Geometric Constant**

Define the material-dependent constant $\beta$ as:

$$
\beta \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \pi \, k \, \rho \, C_p \, (T_m - T_0)}{P}} \quad \text{(5)}
$$

Then the compact form of the **melt-pool width equation** is:

$$
\boxed{\,w \;=\; 2 \, \beta \, v\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Melt-pool width grows **linearly with scan speed** (counter-intuitive — but true for 2-D Rosenthal analysis). In practice, at very high $v$ the assumption breaks down and the width plateaus.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Volumetric Energy Density for LENS

**Step 1 — Volume of One Scan Track per Unit Layer**

For a single scan pass of length $L$, with hatch spacing $h$ and layer thickness $t$, the material volume per layer is:

$$
V_{layer} \;=\; L \cdot h \cdot t \quad \text{(6)}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy Delivered to That Volume**

The laser delivers energy $E = P \cdot \Delta t$, where $\Delta t$ is the dwell time to traverse length $L$ at scan speed $v$, i.e., $\Delta t = L / v$:

$$
E \;=\; P \cdot \dfrac{L}{v} \quad \text{(7)}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the Energy Density Ratio**

$$
E_v \;=\; \dfrac{E}{V_{layer}} \;=\; \dfrac{P \cdot L / v}{L \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \text{(8)}
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel Common Terms**

$L$ cancels out:

$$
\boxed{\,E_v \;=\; \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Typical Value Window for defect-free LENS of Ti-6Al-4V:** $E_v$ between **60 J/mm³ and 120 J/mm³**. Below this range → *lack of fusion porosity*. Above this → *keyhole porosity and vaporization*.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Deposition Rate vs. Powder Mass Flow

Let $\dot{m}_{feed}$ be the powder feed rate (g/s) supplied by the feeder. The **mass deposition rate** onto the substrate is reduced by the **catchment efficiency** $\eta$:

$$
\dot{m}_{deposited} \;=\; \eta \cdot \dot{m}_{feed} \quad \text{(9)}
$$

The volumetric deposition rate, knowing the powder bulk density $\rho_{bulk}$ of the loose powder:

$$
\dot{V} \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \cdot \dot{m}_{feed}}{\rho_{bulk}} \quad \text{(10)}
$$

For a single scan track of cross-section $A_{track} = h \cdot t$, the corresponding **maximum traverse speed** to consume this volume in a single layer is:

$$
v_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{V}}{h \cdot t} \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \cdot \dot{m}_{feed}}{\rho_{bulk} \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \text{(11)}
$$

This couples **powder flow engineering** with **motion-control engineering** — a recurring KTU conceptual question.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defect vs. Process Parameter

| Defect Mode | Cause (Parameter Effect) | Remedy |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Lack of Fusion** | $v$ too high OR $P$ too low → $E_v < 60$ J/mm³ | Reduce scan speed; increase power |
| **Keyholing** | $v$ too low OR $P$ too high → $E_v > 200$ J/mm³ | Increase scan speed; reduce power |
| **Ballistic Defects** | Powder misses melt pool (η low) | Adjust stand-off distance (~10 mm); align nozzles |
| **Oxidation** | O₂ > 50 ppm in chamber | Improve argon purge; check seals |
| **Delamination** | Layer thickness > melt depth / 2 | Reduce $t$; pre-heat substrate |
| **Residual Stress** | High thermal gradient, no pre-heat | Substrate pre-heat to 200 – 400 °C |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — LENS Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CAD File STL] --> B[Slicer Software]
    B --> C[G-code / Toolpath]
    C --> D[Multi-axis CNC Gantry]
    D --> E[Coaxial Deposition Head]
    F[Nd YAG Fiber Laser 200W to 4kW] --> G[Optical Fiber]
    G --> E
    H[Powder Hopper] --> I[Mass Flow Controller]
    I --> J[Carrier Gas Argon]
    J --> E
    E --> K[Melt Pool on Substrate]
    K --> L[Solidified Track]
    L --> M[Build Platform Z stage]
    M --> N[Next Layer Deposit]
    N --> O[3D Metal Part]
    P[IR Pyrometer] --> Q[Closed Loop Controller]
    Q --> F
    Q --> I
    R[Argon Glove Box O2 less than 50 ppm] --> E
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence — Layer-by-Layer Deposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1 Substrate placed on Z stage] --> step2[Step 2 Chamber purged with Argon]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Laser focused on substrate]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 Powder injected coaxially]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5 Melt pool forms]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6 Track solidifies]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7 Z stage raised by layer thickness t]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8 Next layer deposited with 30 percent remelt]
    step8 --> step9{All layers done?}
    step9 -->|No| step3
    step9 -->|Yes| step10[Step 9 Cool and Post Process]
    step10 --> step11[Step 10 Final Machining and HIP]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison — LENS vs Powder Bed Fusion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LENS["LENS DED Process"]
        L1[Powder injected at focal point]
        L2[No pre-spread bed required]
        L3[Multi axis 4 to 5 axis capable]
        L4[Repair and coating friendly]
    end
    subgraph PBF["PBF Process"]
        P1[Powder pre-spread across entire bed]
        P2[Recoater blade or roller used]
        P3[Limited to 3 axis vertical build]
        P4[Not suitable for repair]
    end
    LENS --- PBF
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description of the Coaxial Deposition Head

The optical and pneumatic paths share a common central axis:

- **Central channel:** Laser beam passes through a ZnSe focusing lens (focal length ≈ 100 mm).
- **Three or four outer channels:** Powder + carrier gas jets converge at the focal point with a typical **nozzle angle of 30° – 45°** from the beam axis.
- **Nozzle tip stand-off distance from substrate:** **8 – 12 mm**.
- **Shield gas shroud:** A separate outer ring of argon protects the melt pool from atmospheric ingress.

> [!NOTE]
> This "coaxial" geometry is what allows LENS to deposit on **sloped or curved existing surfaces**, not just flat substrates — the key advantage over PBF.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** 
*Define Laser Engineering Net Shaping (LENS). List any **four** distinguishing features of LENS compared to Selective Laser Melting (SLM).*

**Model Answer (12 – 15 lines):**

LENS is a Directed Energy Deposition (DED) additive manufacturing process in which a focused high-power laser beam creates a localized melt pool on a substrate while metallic powder is simultaneously injected through coaxial nozzles into the molten zone, building dense metallic parts layer-by-layer under a 4- or 5-axis motion platform.

Four distinguishing features vs SLM:

1. **Feedstock delivery** — powder is injected at the focal point (DED), not pre-spread on a bed (PBF).
2. **Multi-axis capability** — LENS can deposit on inclined/curved surfaces; SLM is restricted to horizontal layers.
3. **Repair & coating** — LENS is ideal for repair/additive cladding; SLM cannot repair existing parts.
4. **Build envelope & speed** — LENS has higher deposition rate (up to 0.5 kg/hr) but coarser resolution (≥ 0.5 mm feature size) compared to SLM (≤ 0.2 mm).
5. **Chamber atmosphere** — both need inert atmosphere, but LENS uses a sealed glove box since powder is airborne.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the role of **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** in LENS. State the typical range for defect-free deposition of Ti-6Al-4V.*

**Model Answer (10 – 12 lines):**

Volumetric Energy Density (VED) is the laser energy delivered per unit volume of deposited material:

$$
E_v \;=\; \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

It governs the metallurgical quality of the deposit. If $E_v$ is **too low** (< 60 J/mm³), the powder does not fully melt, producing **lack-of-fusion porosity** and weak inter-layer bonding. If $E_v$ is **too high** (> 200 J/mm³), the metal vaporizes deeply, forming **keyhole pores** and excessive spatter.

**Typical defect-free range for Ti-6Al-4V:** **60 – 120 J/mm³**.

VED therefore acts as the **primary process-window parameter** in LENS, and closed-loop controllers monitor it in real time.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]*

**(a)** Describe the complete **working principle of LENS** with a neat block diagram of the system. Discuss the role of each sub-system. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** and explain how each parameter influences the **microstructure and defect formation** in LENS deposits. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Introduction (2 marks):**
LENS (Laser Engineering Net Shaping) is a DED-AM process developed by Sandia National Labs (USA) for fabricating fully dense metallic components. Unlike powder bed fusion, the metal powder is delivered directly to the laser-substrate interaction zone through a coaxial nozzle arrangement.

**System Description (4 marks):**
The LENS system comprises five interconnected sub-systems:

| Sub-System | Function |
|:--|:--|
| Laser Source (Nd:YAG/Fiber, 200 W – 4 kW) | Provides the melting energy, delivered via optical fiber |
| Powder Feeder (gravimetric) | Meters powder at 5 – 30 g/min into carrier gas stream |
| Motion Platform (4/5-axis CNC or robot) | Positions deposition head along programmed toolpath |
| Atmosphere Control (glove box + Ar purge) | Maintains O₂ < 50 ppm to prevent oxidation |
| Process Monitoring (pyrometer / IR camera) | Provides closed-loop feedback on melt-pool temperature |

**Working (1 mark):**
The focused laser creates a stationary melt pool on the substrate. Powder, entrained in argon carrier gas, is injected coaxially and absorbed by the melt pool. As the head traverses, the molten metal solidifies forming a dense track. Layer thickness increments of 0.25 – 0.5 mm are added successively.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid block diagram.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation (4 marks):**
For a track length $L$ scanned at speed $v$:

- Time to scan: $\Delta t = L / v$
- Energy delivered: $E = P \cdot (L/v)$
- Volume of one layer: $V = L \cdot h \cdot t$
- Energy density: $E_v = E / V = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$

**Influence on microstructure and defects (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Increase Effect | Decrease Effect |
|:--|:--|:--|
| $P$ | Deeper penetration; keyhole risk at high $P$ | Shallower melting; lack-of-fusion risk |
| $v$ | Faster build but smaller melt pool | More energy per unit length; remelt zone grows |
| $h$ | Coarser scan; may leave porosity between tracks | Smoother surface; slower build |
| $t$ | Faster build; risk of incomplete re-melt | Thinner layers; finer resolution |

**Conclusion (0 marks internal):**
Maintaining $E_v$ in the alloy-specific sweet spot is the **golden rule** of LENS process design.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the **powder catchment efficiency** in LENS. Discuss the factors affecting it and its impact on deposition rate. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** With suitable sketches, compare **LENS, Electron Beam Additive Manufacturing (EBAM), and Wire-Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM)** based on energy source, feedstock, deposition rate, accuracy, and typical applications. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Powder catchment efficiency $\eta$ is defined as the ratio of powder mass actually melted into the deposit to the total powder mass injected through the nozzles:

$$
\eta \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_{deposited}}{\dot{m}_{injected}}
$$

Typical values for LENS: **30 % – 70 %**.

**Factors affecting catchment efficiency (4 marks):**

1. **Stand-off distance (nozzle-to-substrate):** Optimal at 8 – 12 mm; too close → turbulence; too far → powder misses melt pool.
2. **Nozzle angle:** 30° – 45° from beam axis concentrates powder at focal point.
3. **Carrier gas flow rate:** High flow scatters powder; low flow causes clogging.
4. **Melt-pool size:** Larger pool catches more powder but lowers resolution.
5. **Substrate orientation:** Vertical or inclined surfaces have lower η than horizontal.
6. **Powder size distribution:** 45 – 150 µm spherical powders give best results.

**Impact on deposition rate (1 mark):**
Deposition rate $= \eta \cdot \dot{m}_{feed}$. Improving η from 30 % to 60 % doubles the effective build speed without increasing feeder cost.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Feature | LENS | EBAM | WAAM |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Energy Source** | Laser (Nd:YAG/Fiber) | Electron Beam (vacuum) | Electric Arc (GMAW/PAW) |
| **Feedstock** | Metal powder (45 – 150 µm) | Metal wire (0.8 – 2.4 mm) | Metal wire (1.0 – 3.2 mm) |
| **Atmosphere** | Ar glove box | High vacuum (10⁻⁴ Pa) | Inert shielding gas |
| **Deposition Rate** | 0.05 – 0.5 kg/hr | 2 – 9 kg/hr | 1 – 10 kg/hr |
| **Accuracy** | High (± 0.1 mm) | Medium (± 0.5 mm) | Low (≥ 1 mm), needs machining |
| **Typical Materials** | Ti, Inconel, tool steel | Ti-6Al-4V (aerospace) | Mild steel, SS, Al alloys |
| **Build Volume** | Small–medium | Very large | Very large |
| **Applications** | Aerospace repair, medical implants | Large aerospace structural parts | Ship hulls, pressure vessels |
| **Cost** | High | Very high | Low |

**Conclusion:** LENS offers the **best balance of accuracy and material flexibility**, EBAM excels in **vacuum-required reactive metals**, and WAAM dominates in **large low-cost structures**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in KTU LENS answers:**
> 1. **Confusing LENS with SLM** — students often write "powder is spread on a bed" — this loses 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting units in VED derivation** — J/mm³ must appear in the final box.
> 3. **Omitting the inert atmosphere** — examiner expects explicit mention of Ar glove box and O₂ < 50 ppm.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** for Rosenthal-type derivations (steady-state, constant properties, 2-D heat flow).
> 5. **Writing "LENS is the same as DMLS"** — DMLS is a powder bed process; LENS is DED. Always check.
> 6. **Missing the multi-axis advantage** — many answers omit the 4/5-axis repair capability which is THE key differentiator.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- LENS = **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** with **laser + injected powder**.
- Developed by **Sandia National Labs**; commercialized by **Optomec**.
- Powder is delivered **coaxially**, not spread on a bed.
- **VED = P / (v · h · t)** — primary process-window metric.
- Ti-6Al-4V sweet spot: **60 – 120 J/mm³**.
- **Nd:YAG or Yb-fiber laser** at **200 W – 4 kW**.
- Build chamber must have **O₂ < 50 ppm** under argon.
- **4-axis / 5-axis** motion = repair and cladding capability.
- **Catchment efficiency η = 30 – 70 %** — affected by stand-off, nozzle angle, gas flow.
- **Closed-loop control** uses IR pyrometer to monitor melt pool.
- Cooling rate **10³ – 10⁵ K/s** → fine martensitic / dendritic microstructure.
- **Post-processing:** HIP and CNC machining often required.
- LENS cannot match SLM resolution (< 0.5 mm vs < 0.2 mm) but wins on **deposition rate**.
- ASTM F2792 / ISO/ASTM 52900 category: **DED-LB** (Directed Energy Deposition – Laser Beam).
- Always cite **energy density units in J/mm³** for full marks.
- Key defects: **lack-of-fusion** (low E_v) and **keyholing** (high E_v).
- Major OEMs besides Optomec: **Trumpf (TruLaser Cell), DMG MORI (LASERTEC), BeAM, ADDUP (FORMIGA)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LIGA Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT415 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** LIGA Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**LIGA** is a German acronym derived from the core steps of the process:
- **L**ithographie (Lithography)
- **G**alvanoformung (Electroplating / Electroforming)
- **A**bformung (Molding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** LIGA is a micro-fabrication / micro-manufacturing process used to produce high-aspect-ratio (HAR) micro-structures and micro-components by combining **deep X-ray lithography**, **electroforming**, and **precision injection/compression molding**. It is a non-conventional additive-subtractive hybrid technique that bridges semiconductor lithography and macro-scale polymer processing.

In the context of **Additive Manufacturing**, LIGA is classified as a **mask-based, layer-by-layer (essentially monolithic) AM process for micro-scale parts** and is frequently listed under the broader category of *"Micro-AM / Solid Freeform Fabrication of Microsystems."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The "Micro-Sandwich & Stamp" method:**
> Imagine you want to print a tiny, 3-D coin-stack shaped logo on a plastic ruler:
> 1. You first carve the negative of that logo into a **wax block** using a powerful light (this is the lithography step).
> 2. You then dip the carved wax into a bath of molten **nickel** so that nickel fills up every tiny cavity — like pouring metal into a chocolate mould (this is the electroforming step).
> 3. Finally, you remove the wax, and you have a **metallic master mould (the stamp)**. You can press this stamp thousands of times into soft plastic to mass-produce microscopic plastic parts (this is the moulding step).
> 
> That's LIGA — make a master with X-rays, replicate it in metal, and mass-produce it in plastic!

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Performance Indices

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Structural Height (h)** | **10 µm – 3 mm** | Uniquely tall for a lithographic process |
| **Aspect Ratio (h/w)** | **Up to 100 : 1** | Tallest among all AM/litho processes |
| **Lateral Resolution (w)** | **0.2 – 5 µm** | Sub-micron features possible |
| **Sidewall Roughness (Ra)** | **30 – 100 nm** | Optical-grade finish |
| **Synchrotron X-ray Energy** | **1 – 5 keV (soft) to > 20 keV** | High penetration depth |
| **Standard Resist Material** | **PMMA (Polymethyl Methacrylate)** | Positive X-ray resist |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of LIGA — Step-by-Step Logic

The LIGA process is a **sequence of three physical transformations**, each converting the output of the previous step into the input of the next.

#### **Pillar 1 — Deep X-Ray Lithography (Lithographie)**

- **Why?** Conventional UV lithography (used in IC chips) cannot penetrate more than a few microns of resist. To build **truly 3-D microstructures**, we need a radiation source with very short wavelength and high photon energy.
- **How?** A **synchrotron radiation source** (or, in lower-cost variants, a UV source — called *UV-LIGA*) is used to expose a thick layer of **PMMA resist** coated on an electrically conductive substrate (typically a metallised silicon wafer or a copper plate).
- The X-rays pass through a **high-contrast gold/beryllium X-ray mask** that carries the 2-D pattern of the desired cross-section. Areas exposed to X-rays undergo chain scission (molecular bond breaking) in the PMMA, making them soluble in a developer (GG developer — mixture of water, morpholine, ethanolamine, etc.).

#### **Pillar 2 — Electroforming / Electroplating (Galvanoformung)**

- **Why?** After development, we have a **polymer (PMMA) template with empty cavities** sitting on a conductive substrate. PMMA is mechanically weak and thermally unstable — it cannot be used as a final product. We need a metallic replica.
- **How?** The wafer is immersed in an **electrolytic bath** (typically nickel sulphamate bath, Ni(SO₃NH₂)₂). The conductive base acts as the cathode, and a pure nickel anode dissolves, depositing Ni²⁺ ions into the lithographic cavities. The deposition continues until the cavities are completely filled and even slightly over-filled, creating a free-standing metallic layer.

#### **Pillar 3 — Molding / Replication (Abformung)**

- **Why?** Each X-ray exposure and electroforming cycle is **expensive and slow** (synchrotron time costs hundreds of dollars per hour). The metallic micro-structure produced in Pillar 2 is, in fact, a **master mould insert** — a "negative" of the final desired plastic part.
- **How?** This master is mounted into a **micro-injection moulding** or **hot-embossing** machine, where molten thermoplastic (PMMA, POM, COC, LCP) is forced into the cavities under high pressure. After cooling and ejection, the plastic micro-part is released. The metallic master can be reused **thousands of times**, drastically reducing per-part cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Product Routes in LIGA:**
> 1. **Direct LIGA part** — the electroformed metal itself is the final product (used in micro-fluidics, micro-optics).
> 2. **Indirect LIGA part** — the molded polymer replica is the final product (mass production route).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Aspect Ratio (AR)** | $AR = \dfrac{h}{w}$ | dimensionless | Quality metric of LIGA structures |
| 2 | **Lateral Resolution** | $w_{\min} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA}$ | m | Resolution limit of optical mask aligner |
| 3 | **X-ray Attenuation (Beer-Lambert)** | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \rho \, t}$ | — | Penetration depth in PMMA resist |
| 4 | **X-ray Mass Absorption Coeff. (PMMA)** | $\mu/\rho \approx 1.0 \times 10^3 \text{ cm}^2/\text{g}$ at 1 keV | cm²/g | Determines resist thickness limit |
| 5 | **Dose Required for PMMA Exposure** | $D_{\text{clear}} \approx 4 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$ | J/cm³ | Synchrotron exposure time calculation |
| 6 | **Faraday's Law of Electroplating** | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | g | Mass of Ni deposited per unit time |
| 7 | **Nickel Deposition Rate** | $r = \dfrac{j \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_{Ni}}$ | µm/h | Vertical growth velocity of Ni plug |
| 8 | **Current Density (typical)** | $j = 1 - 20 \text{ A/dm}^2$ | A/dm² | Sulphamate bath operating range |
| 9 | **Shrinkage on Moulding** | $\varepsilon_s = \dfrac{L_{\text{mould}} - L_{\text{part}}}{L_{\text{mould}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Dimensional compensation |
| 10 | **Demoulding Force (Friction)** | $F_d = \tau \cdot A_{\text{side}}$ | N | Force to release plastic from Ni mould |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **$\mu$** = linear absorption coefficient (cm⁻¹), **$\rho$** = density (g/cm³), **$t$** = thickness (cm)
> - **$M$** = molar mass (Ni = 58.69 g/mol), **$n$** = valency (Ni²⁺ = 2), **$F$** = 96,485 C/mol
> - **$j$** = current density (A/m²), **$\tau$** = interfacial shear stress (Pa), **$A$** = side-wall area (m²)

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of LIGA |
|---|---|
| **MEMS / Microsystems** | Micro-gears, comb drives, acceleration sensors |
| **Micro-fluidics** | Lab-on-a-chip channels, nozzle arrays, inkjet printer heads |
| **Micro-optics** | Fibre alignment ferrules, micro-lens arrays, V-grooves for fibre ribbons |
| **Telecommunications** | WDM (Wavelength Division Multiplexing) components, photonic crystals |
| **Biomedical** | Micro-needles for drug delivery, neural probes, cell-sorting chips |
| **Automotive** | Pressure sensors, fuel injector micro-nozzles |
| **Aerospace** | Micro-thrusters, micro-reaction chambers |

> [!NOTE]
> The **LETI (France) and KIT (Germany)** research institutes are globally recognised for industrial-grade LIGA development. Companies like **Bosch, STMicroelectronics, and TE Connectivity** use LIGA-derived processes in mass production.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Process Flow (with Equations)

The complete LIGA process flow has **seven sequential sub-steps** that must be understood for KTU 14-mark answers.

#### **Step 1 — Substrate Preparation**

A polished **silicon wafer** (or copper plate) is coated with a thin **seed layer** of conductive metal (e.g., 50 nm Ti + 100 nm Cu) using **sputtering** or **evaporation**. The seed layer is the conductive cathode for electroplating.

The required seed layer resistance is:
$$R_{\text{seed}} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{seed}}}{t_{\text{seed}}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{A_{\text{wafer}}} \;\; [\Omega]$$

where $\rho_{\text{seed}}$ is resistivity (e.g., Cu ≈ 1.7 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m), $t_{\text{seed}}$ is thickness, and $A_{\text{wafer}}$ is wafer area. Low $R_{\text{seed}}$ is required for **uniform current distribution** in the electroforming bath.

#### **Step 2 — PMMA Resist Coating**

PMMA is dissolved in an organic solvent and **spin-coated** (for thin films, ≤ 50 µm) or **laminated** as solid sheets (for thick films, ≥ 100 µm). The wet resist is then soft-baked to remove solvent.

Soft-bake evaporation rate follows:
$$\dfrac{dM}{dt} = -k \cdot (M_0 - M_{\text{eq}})$$

where $M$ is residual solvent mass, $M_0$ is initial mass, $M_{\text{eq}}$ is equilibrium residual mass, and $k$ is the desorption rate constant (Arrhenius-dependent on bake temperature).

#### **Step 3 — X-Ray Exposure through Mask**

A **synchrotron** generates high-brilliance X-rays with critical energy $E_c$:
$$E_c \;[\text{keV}] = 2.218 \cdot \dfrac{E_{\text{ring}}^3}{\rho_{\text{magnet}} \cdot R} \approx 0.665 \cdot B \cdot E_{\text{ring}}^2$$

For typical bending-magnet synchrotrons, $E_c$ lies in the range of 0.5 – 5 keV, ideal for PMMA.

The X-ray intensity at depth $t$ inside PMMA is:
$$I(t) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_{\text{PMMA}} \cdot t}$$

The absorbed dose at the bottom of the resist (depth $h$) is:
$$D(h) = \dfrac{I_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-\mu_{\text{PMMA}} \cdot h}) \cdot t_{\text{exp}} \cdot E_{\text{ph}}}{\rho_{\text{PMMA}} \cdot h}$$

For complete chain scission, $D(h) \geq D_{\text{clear}} = 4 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$. If $D(h) < D_{\text{clear}}$, the bottom of the resist is **under-exposed** and will not dissolve, causing **incomplete development**.

#### **Step 4 — Development**

The exposed wafer is immersed in the **GG developer** at 25–35 °C with mild agitation. The developer dissolves the exposed PMMA in a diffusion-limited process. The dissolution depth $z(t)$ follows:
$$z(t) = k_d \cdot \sqrt{t_{\text{dev}}}$$

where $k_d$ is the dissolution rate constant (typically 0.3 – 1.0 µm/min for properly exposed PMMA in GG developer at 30 °C).

Sidewall verticality depends on the **penumbra effect**:
$$\delta_{\text{penumbra}} = \dfrac{d \cdot s}{h_{\text{gap}}}$$

where $d$ is the X-ray source size, $s$ is the resist-mask distance, and $h_{\text{gap}}$ is the gap between mask and resist. To minimise $\delta_{\text{penumbra}}$, synchrotron sources are designed with $d \approx 100 \text{ µm}$ and proximity exposure is performed with $s \approx 10 - 100 \text{ µm}$.

#### **Step 5 — Electroforming (Galvanoformung)**

The wafer is now mounted in an **electroforming cell**. The seed layer is connected to the negative terminal of a DC power supply; a pure Ni basket is the anode.

Mass of nickel deposited is given by **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis**:
$$m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t_{\text{dep}}}{n \cdot F}$$

The corresponding thickness grown vertically is:
$$h_{\text{Ni}} = \dfrac{m}{\rho_{\text{Ni}} \cdot A_{\text{deposit}}} = \dfrac{M \cdot j \cdot t_{\text{dep}}}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_{\text{Ni}}}$$

For nickel:
- $M_{\text{Ni}} = 58.69 \text{ g/mol}$
- $n = 2$
- $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$
- $\rho_{\text{Ni}} = 8.908 \text{ g/cm}^3$

Substituting:
$$h_{\text{Ni}} \;[\mu m] = 0.0194 \cdot j \cdot t_{\text{dep}}$$

where $j$ is in A/dm² and $t_{\text{dep}}$ is in hours.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example:** For a 500 µm tall Ni plug using $j = 5 \text{ A/dm}^2$:
> $$t_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{500}{0.0194 \cdot 5} = 5155 \text{ hours} \approx 7.2 \text{ months}$$
> This shows why **LIGA electroforming is the rate-limiting step** and why most processes either use a lower $h$ or higher $j$ (up to 20 A/dm²) — reducing the time to ~50 days.

#### **Step 6 — Resist Removal and Mold Release**

PMMA is stripped using a strong solvent (e.g., **dichloromethane** or **acetone**). The Ni structure remains bonded to the substrate. If a free-standing Ni insert is desired, the substrate is etched away (KOH for Si, FeCl₃ for Cu).

#### **Step 7 — Molding (Abformung)**

The Ni insert is mounted in a **micro-injection-moulding machine** (e.g., Battenfeld, Sumitomo). Molten polymer (POM, PMMA, COC) at temperature $T_{\text{melt}} \approx 200 - 280 \text{°C}$ is injected at pressure $P_{\text{inj}} = 50 - 200 \text{ MPa}$.

The **cycle time** is:
$$t_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\text{inject}} + t_{\text{cool}} + t_{\text{eject}}$$

The cooling time is given by:
$$t_{\text{cool}} = \dfrac{h^2}{\pi^2 \cdot \alpha_{\text{th}}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_{\text{melt}} - T_{\text{mould}}}{T_{\text{eject}} - T_{\text{mould}}}\right)$$

where $\alpha_{\text{th}}$ is thermal diffusivity of the polymer, $T_{\text{mould}}$ is mould temperature, and $T_{\text{eject}}$ is the ejection temperature.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Variants of LIGA

| Variant | Radiation Source | Resist | Max h (µm) | Max AR | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **X-Ray LIGA (Standard)** | Synchrotron | PMMA | 3000 | 100 : 1 | Very High |
| **UV-LIGA** | Mercury lamp | SU-8, EPON | 200 | 20 : 1 | Moderate |
| **SIL-STEP (Steward)** | UV lamp | UV-resin | 100 | 10 : 1 | Low |
| **Laser-LIGA** | Excimer laser | Dry film | 50 | 5 : 1 | Low |
| **e-beam LIGA** | E-beam | PMMA | 100 | 30 : 1 | High |

### 3.3 Critical Process Parameter Matrix (for KTU Numerical Problems)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect if ↑ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Exposure dose | $D$ | 4 – 8 kJ/cm³ | Over-exposure → under-cutting |
| Synchrotron energy | $E_{\text{ph}}$ | 1 – 5 keV | Higher → deeper penetration |
| Current density | $j$ | 1 – 20 A/dm² | Higher → faster deposition, rougher Ni |
| Bath temperature | $T_{\text{bath}}$ | 45 – 60 °C | Higher → larger grain size |
| pH of bath | pH | 3.5 – 4.5 | Lower → hydrogen embrittlement |
| Mask-substrate gap | $s$ | 10 – 100 µm | Higher → blurrier sidewalls |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Full LIGA Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Conductive Substrate] --> B[Sputter Ti Cu Seed Layer]
    B --> C[Coat Thick PMMA Resist via Spin or Lamination]
    C --> D[Soft Bake to Remove Solvent]
    D --> E[Synchrotron X-ray Exposure through Au X-ray Mask]
    E --> F[Develop in GG Developer - Dissolves Exposed PMMA]
    F --> G{Resist Pattern Complete?}
    G -- No --> E
    G -- Yes --> H[Electroform Ni in Sulphamate Bath]
    H --> I[Ni Fills PMMA Cavities - Overplate Slightly]
    I --> J[Strip PMMA with Solvent]
    J --> K[Etch Substrate if Free Standing Insert Needed]
    K --> L[Ni Master Insert Ready]
    L --> M[Micro Injection Mold Polymer Pellets]
    M --> N[Cool and Eject Plastic Micro Part]
    N --> O[Final LIGA Replicated Micro Component]
    L --> P[OR Use Ni Insert Directly as Functional Part]
    P --> O
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an LIGA System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXPOSURE[Exposure Subsystem]
        SYN[Synchrotron Electron Storage Ring] --> BM[Bending Magnet]
        BM --> BE[Beamline Filter]
        BE --> MSK[Au X-ray Mask]
        MSK --> WAF[PMMA coated Wafer]
    end
    subgraph DEV[Development Subsystem]
        WAF --> BATH1[GG Developer Bath with Agitator]
        BATH1 --> RINSE1[DI Water Rinse]
        RINSE1 --> DRY1[Spin Dry]
    end
    subgraph FORM[Electroforming Subsystem]
        DRY1 --> TANK[Ni Sulphamate Electrolytic Tank]
        TANK --> PS[DC Power Supply]
        PS --> MON[pH and Temperature Controller]
    end
    subgraph MOLD[Molding Subsystem]
        TANK --> INS[Ni Master Insert]
        INS --> IMM[Micro Injection Molding Machine]
        IMM --> PEL[Polymer Pellets Feed]
        IMM --> OUT[Replicated Micro Parts]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Tree — LIGA Product Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[LIGA Process Output] --> M1[Direct Metal Micro Part - Ni]
    L --> M2[Indirect Polymer Replica]
    M1 --> U1[Micro Fluidic Nozzles]
    M1 --> U2[Micro Optics Ferrules]
    M1 --> U3[MEMS Comb Drives]
    M2 --> U4[Lab on Chip Channels]
    M2 --> U5[Micro Gears and Springs]
    M2 --> U6[Micro Needles for Drug Delivery]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Expand the acronym LIGA and explain the meaning of each component.**

**Model Answer:**
LIGA is a German acronym for:
- **L — Lithographie (Lithography):** Deep X-ray lithography is used to expose a thick PMMA resist using synchrotron radiation through a patterned X-ray mask, creating a 3-D polymer template.
- **G — Galvanoformung (Electroplating):** The lithographic cavities are filled with a metal (typically nickel) by electroforming from a sulphamate bath, producing a metallic master insert.
- **A — Abformung (Molding):** The Ni master is used as an insert in micro-injection-moulding or hot-embossing equipment to mass-produce polymer replicas of the original pattern.

*(3 marks — 1 for expansion, 1 for explaining L, 1 for G and A combined)*

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **List any two advantages and one limitation of the LIGA process.**

**Model Answer:**
**Advantages:**
1. Very high aspect ratio (up to 100 : 1) compared to other micro-fabrication techniques.
2. Excellent sidewall verticality (≈ 89.9°) and optical-grade surface finish (Ra ≈ 30 – 100 nm).
3. Mass replication capability — one master insert can produce millions of identical plastic parts.
4. Compatible with metals, polymers, and ceramics.

**Limitations:**
1. Requires a synchrotron radiation source, which is **extremely expensive** (≈ 1 – 2 million dollars per day of beam time) and not widely accessible.

*(3 marks — 1 mark each for advantage, advantage, limitation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Q3A.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3] **(a)** Explain the complete LIGA process with a neat flowchart describing the seven major steps, and **(b)** derive the expression for the thickness of nickel deposited during the electroforming step, highlighting the key process parameters.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks**

The LIGA process consists of the following seven sequential steps:

1. **Substrate preparation:** A polished silicon wafer or copper plate is coated with a thin conductive seed layer (Ti/Cu, 50/100 nm) by sputtering. This acts as the cathode for the electroforming step.

2. **PMMA resist coating:** A thick layer (100 – 1000 µm) of polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) is deposited on the substrate by spin-coating (thin layers) or sheet lamination (thick layers). The resist is soft-baked to remove residual solvent.

3. **Synchrotron X-ray exposure:** The PMMA-coated wafer is exposed to synchrotron-generated X-rays (1 – 5 keV) through a patterned gold-on-beryllium X-ray mask. Exposed regions of PMMA undergo chain scission and become soluble.

4. **Development:** The exposed wafer is immersed in the GG developer (a mixture of 2-(2-aminoethoxy)ethanol, morpholine, and water) at 30 °C. The exposed PMMA dissolves out, leaving behind a 3-D polymer template on the conductive substrate.

5. **Electroforming:** The wafer is placed in a nickel sulphamate bath. With the seed layer as cathode, Ni²⁺ ions from the bath are reduced and deposited into the cavities, growing vertically until the entire resist template is filled and over-plated.

6. **Resist stripping and substrate release:** The remaining PMMA is dissolved in dichloromethane. If a free-standing Ni insert is needed, the underlying Si is etched with KOH.

7. **Molding (Abformung):** The Ni master is mounted in a micro-injection-moulding machine. Molten thermoplastic is injected at high pressure (50 – 200 MPa) and temperature (200 – 280 °C), cooled, and ejected. This produces a polymer replica of the original X-ray pattern.

> *[Examiner's note: Flowchart should be drawn on the answer script for full 7 marks. Each step carries 1 mark.]*

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks**

The deposition of nickel during electroforming follows **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis**:
$$m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t_{\text{dep}}}{n \cdot F}$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of Ni deposited (g)
- $M$ = molar mass of Ni = 58.69 g/mol
- $I$ = current passed (A)
- $t_{\text{dep}}$ = deposition time (s)
- $n$ = valency of Ni²⁺ = 2
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol

The thickness of the deposit is obtained by dividing $m$ by the product of Ni density $\rho_{\text{Ni}}$ and deposit cross-sectional area $A$:
$$h_{\text{Ni}} = \dfrac{m}{\rho_{\text{Ni}} \cdot A} = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t_{\text{dep}}}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_{\text{Ni}} \cdot A}$$

Using the definition of **current density** $j = I/A$, this simplifies to:
$$h_{\text{Ni}} = \dfrac{M \cdot j \cdot t_{\text{dep}}}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_{\text{Ni}}}$$

Substituting Ni values:
$$h_{\text{Ni}} = \dfrac{58.69 \times j \times t_{\text{dep}}}{2 \times 96485 \times 8.908} \;\; [\text{cm}]$$

**Numerical Simplification:**
$$h_{\text{Ni}} \;[\mu m] = 0.0194 \times j \;[\text{A/dm}^2] \times t_{\text{dep}} \;[\text{h}]$$

**Conclusion / Key Insights:**
- A current density of $j = 5 \text{ A/dm}^2$ deposits Ni at approximately **0.097 µm/h** — extremely slow.
- For a 500 µm tall structure, deposition time is approximately **5150 hours (~ 7.2 months)**.
- This is the **rate-limiting step** of LIGA. Strategies to reduce time include:
  - Increasing $j$ (e.g., to 20 A/dm² → 0.388 µm/h).
  - Using pulsed current for better throwing power.
  - Increasing bath temperature (45 – 60 °C).

---

> **Q3B.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3] **(a)** Compare the LIGA process with UV-LIGA and explain the role of synchrotron radiation in deep X-ray lithography, and **(b)** enumerate the major industrial applications of LIGA in MEMS, microfluidics, and micro-optics, with one example for each domain.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Parameter | **Standard LIGA (X-ray)** | **UV-LIGA** |
|---|---|---|
| Radiation source | Synchrotron X-rays | Mercury UV lamp (i / h line, 365 – 405 nm) |
| Wavelength | ≈ 0.1 – 1 nm (hard / soft X-ray) | ≈ 365 nm |
| Photon energy | 1 – 20 keV | 3.4 eV |
| Resist | PMMA | SU-8, EPON SU-8 (negative tone) |
| Maximum resist thickness | up to 3000 µm | up to 200 µm |
| Maximum aspect ratio | **100 : 1** | 20 : 1 |
| Sidewall verticality | ≈ 89.9° | ≈ 88 – 89° |
| Resolution | 0.2 µm | 1 – 2 µm |
| Equipment cost | **Very High** (synchrotron) | Moderate (spin coater + mask aligner) |
| Cycle time | Days to weeks | Hours |
| Accessibility | Limited to synchrotron centres | Available in any MEMS lab |

**Role of Synchrotron Radiation in Deep X-Ray Lithography:**
1. **High photon energy / short wavelength** → enables deep penetration into 100s of µm of PMMA with minimal diffraction-induced blurring.
2. **High collimation** → produces nearly parallel rays, resulting in **vertical sidewalls** and high-fidelity pattern transfer.
3. **High brilliance** → short exposure times even for thick resists.
4. **Tunable spectrum** → optimum energy can be selected for the resist–mask combination.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks**

| Domain | Example of LIGA Application |
|---|---|
| **MEMS** | Nickel micro-gears, comb drives, micro-turbines, micro-spring arrays, gyroscopes. Example: Bosch uses LIGA-derived micro-accelerometers in automotive airbag systems. |
| **Microfluidics** | High-aspect-ratio micro-channels for lab-on-a-chip, inkjet printer head nozzle plates, drug delivery micro-needles. Example: Agilent uses LIGA-fabricated inkjet nozzle arrays (12 nozzles / mm) in HPLC systems. |
| **Micro-optics** | Fibre-optic alignment ferrules, V-grooves, micro-lens arrays, photonic crystals. Example: Telecom-grade LIGA ferrule aligns 12-fibre ribbons with < 0.5 µm lateral tolerance. |
| **Biomedical** | Neural probe arrays, micro-surgical tools, micro-tweezers. |
| **Automotive / Aerospace** | Micro-thrusters, pressure sensors, micro-reaction chambers. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in LIGA Answers:**
> 1. **Spelling Trap:** LIGA is German — students often write "GALVANOFO RMUNG" or "LIGIA". Marks are **deducted for incorrect expansion** in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Numerical Mistake:** Many students confuse current density units (A/dm² vs A/m²) in Faraday's law derivation. **1 A/dm² = 100 A/m²** — wrong conversion leads to factor-of-100 errors in calculated thickness.
> 3. **Confusing LIGA with SLA/SLS:** LIGA is **mask-based and uses polymer + metal + moulding**, NOT a photopolymer vat-curing process (SLA) or powder fusion (SLS). Marks lost if written confusingly.
> 4. **Forgetting the X-ray mask:** Students often skip the role of the Au X-ray mask in the lithography step. **Always mention the mask** — it is the patterning element.
> 5. **No flowchart / no diagram in 14-mark Q:** At least **1 well-labelled flowchart** is mandatory for full marks in 7-mark sub-parts describing a process.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — LIGA Process (Module 3)**

- ✅ **LIGA = Lithographie + Galvanoformung + Abformung** (X-ray lithography + Electroplating + Moulding).
- ✅ Classified under **Micro-Additive Manufacturing / Solid Freeform Fabrication** in KTU 2024 syllabus.
- ✅ Three pillars: **Deep X-ray lithography (synchrotron + PMMA), Electroforming (Ni sulphamate bath), Moulding (micro-injection / hot embossing)**.
- ✅ Maximum aspect ratio **100 : 1** — unique among AM processes.
- ✅ Maximum structural height **3 mm** — tallest of all lithographic techniques.
- ✅ Lateral resolution **0.2 – 5 µm**; sidewall roughness **Ra ≈ 30 – 100 nm**.
- ✅ **Faraday's Law** is the governing equation for the electroforming step:
  $$m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}, \quad h = \dfrac{M \cdot j \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$
- ✅ For Ni: $h \, [\mu m] \approx 0.0194 \times j \, [\text{A/dm}^2] \times t \, [\text{h}]$.
- ✅ **Penumbra effect** controls sidewall verticality: $\delta = d \cdot s / h_{\text{gap}}$.
- ✅ **Dose required** for PMMA: $D_{\text{clear}} \approx 4 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$.
- ✅ **Variants:** UV-LIGA (cheaper, lower AR), Laser-LIGA, e-beam LIGA, SIL-STEP.
- ✅ **Two product routes:** Direct (Ni itself) and Indirect (polymer replica).
- ✅ **Applications:** MEMS, microfluidics, micro-optics, biomedical micro-needles, automotive sensors, telecom ferrules.
- ✅ **Key limitation:** Synchrotron source is **expensive and scarce** — drives development of UV-LIGA.
- ✅ **Major centres:** KIT (Germany), LETI (France), ANL (USA).
- ✅ **Standard mould material:** Nickel (deposited from sulphamate bath).
- ✅ **Standard resist material:** PMMA (positive X-ray resist).
- ✅ **Standard developer:** GG developer at 30 °C.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection Laser Melting (SLM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT415 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection Laser Melting (SLM)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** is a powder bed fusion (PBF) based additive manufacturing process that uses a high-energy density **laser beam** to **fully melt and fuse** metallic powder particles layer-by-layer in a **controlled inert atmosphere** (typically Argon or Nitrogen) to produce fully dense, near-net-shape functional metallic components with mechanical properties comparable to wrought materials.

Unlike its cousin **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** which only sinters powder particles (partial fusion), SLM achieves **complete melting** above the melting point of the metal, producing parts with **relative density > 99%** and isotropic mechanical behavior.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Microscopic Welder Painting in 3D":**
> Imagine you have a tray of fine metallic sand (like beach sand, but made of stainless steel or titanium). Now imagine a tiny, super-precise welding torch (the laser) that zaps individual grains of sand, melting them into a solid puddle. After that thin puddle solidifies, someone sprinkles a new layer of sand on top, and the torch does it again — a few micrometres higher. After repeating this millions of times, you have a fully solid metal part that emerged from a pile of dust.
> - The **laser** = microscopic welder
> - The **metal powder** = the building blocks
> - The **inert gas** = blanket that prevents fire/oxidation
> - The **layer-by-layer scan** = "3D painting with molten metal"

## 1.3 Why SLM? — The "Why" Behind Full Melting

| Property | SLS (Sintering) | **SLM (Full Melting)** |
|----------|----------------|------------------------|
| Bonding | Partial fusion | **Complete metallurgical fusion** |
| Density | ~85–95% | **> 99%** |
| Mechanical Strength | Lower | **Comparable to wrought** |
| Surface Finish | Rougher | **Smoother, but post-processing needed** |
| Materials | Polymers, some metals | **Pure metals, alloys, composites** |

## 1.4 Historical Context (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> SLM was pioneered by **Fraunhofer ILT (Germany) in 1995** and later commercialized by companies like **EOS GmbH, SLM Solutions, Renishaw, and 3D Systems**. The trademark **"SLM®"** is technically owned by SLM Solutions, but the term is now used generically in academic literature for the entire process family (also called **LPBF — Laser Powder Bed Fusion** in ISO/ASTM 52900 terminology).

## 1.5 Standard Process Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Laser Power (P):** typically **100 W to 1 kW** (Yb-fiber lasers, wavelength ~1070 nm)
- **Layer Thickness (t):** typically **20 μm to 100 μm**
- **Scan Speed (v):** typically **100 mm/s to 2000 mm/s**
- **Hatch Spacing (h):** typically **50 μm to 200 μm**
- **Build Chamber Oxygen:** maintained at **< 1000 ppm (often < 100 ppm)**
- **Build Volume (industrial):** typically **250 × 250 × 350 mm³**
- **Spot Size:** typically **70 μm to 200 μm**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SLM Process — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The SLM process follows a **cyclic layer-wise protocol**. Each layer consists of **five sequential steps** that repeat thousands of times to build a single part.

### Step 1: Powder Deposition
A **recoater blade** (or roller) spreads a thin, uniform layer of metal powder (typically **20–100 μm thick**) across the **build platform**. The powder reservoir is raised, and the recoater moves horizontally to deposit powder.

### Step 2: Powder Bed Pre-Heating
A **heater plate** beneath the build platform maintains the powder bed at an elevated temperature (typically **80–200 °C**, depending on material) to:
- Reduce thermal gradients
- Minimize residual stress
- Prevent warping

### Step 3: Laser Scanning & Melting
A **Yb-fiber laser** (wavelength ~**1070 nm**) is directed via **galvanometer scanners** and **F-theta lenses** to selectively melt regions of the powder bed according to the sliced CAD geometry. The laser power, scan speed, and hatch pattern are precisely controlled.

### Step 4: Solidification & Cooling
The molten pool **solidifies rapidly** (cooling rates **10⁴–10⁶ K/s**), producing a fine **martensitic or dendritic microstructure** depending on the alloy system. This rapid solidification gives SLM parts their characteristic **high strength** but also induces **residual stress**.

### Step 5: Platform Lowering & Recoating
The build platform descends by **one layer thickness (Δz)**, and the cycle repeats. The entire chamber is sealed and flooded with **inert gas (Ar or N₂)** to maintain oxygen levels **< 1000 ppm** to prevent oxidation and porosity.

## 2.2 Process Parameter Relationships — The "Core Four"

The four most critical process parameters that govern SLM part quality are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Typical Range | Effect on Part Quality |
|-----------|--------|------|---------------|------------------------|
| **Laser Power** | $P$ | W | 100 – 1000 | ↑ Power → deeper melt pool, more density, risk of vaporization |
| **Scan Speed** | $v$ | mm/s | 100 – 2000 | ↑ Speed → less energy input, risk of lack-of-fusion porosity |
| **Hatch Spacing** | $h$ | μm | 50 – 200 | ↑ Spacing → less overlap, risk of inter-track porosity |
| **Layer Thickness** | $t$ | μm | 20 – 100 | ↑ Thickness → less resolution, but faster build |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most important equation in SLM is the **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** equation, which combines all four process parameters into a single metric. KTU examiners **love** this formula.

$$
E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

Where:
- $E_v$ = Volumetric Energy Density (J/mm³)
- $P$ = Laser Power (W)
- $v$ = Scan Speed (mm/s)
- $h$ = Hatch Spacing (mm)
- $t$ = Layer Thickness (mm)

### Typical Energy Density Windows (High-Yield Table)

| Material | Optimal $E_v$ (J/mm³) | Defect if Too Low | Defect if Too High |
|----------|----------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **316L Stainless Steel** | 60 – 100 | Lack of fusion | Keyhole porosity |
| **Ti-6Al-4V** | 40 – 80 | Lack of fusion | Evaporation, residual stress |
| **AlSi10Mg** | 30 – 70 | Porosity | Balling, oxidation |
| **Inconel 718** | 50 – 90 | Lack of fusion | Cracking, distortion |
| **Co-Cr-Mo** | 60 – 110 | Lack of fusion | Keyholing |

## 2.4 Additional KTU High-Yield Formulas

### Build Rate Equation

$$
R_b = v \cdot h \cdot t
$$

Where $R_b$ is the volumetric build rate (mm³/s). Note: this is **exactly the denominator** of the energy density equation — hence the inverse relationship.

### Cooling Rate Estimate (for solidification theory)

$$
\dot{T} \approx \frac{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{P}
$$

Where $k$ is thermal conductivity and $T_m - T_0$ is the undercooling.

### Melt Pool Depth (Approximate Analytical Estimate)

$$
d_{pool} \approx \sqrt{\frac{4 P}{\pi \rho C_p v (T_m - T_0)}}
$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> SLM is a **mission-critical** technology in:
> - **Aerospace:** Lightweight topology-optimized brackets (e.g., Airbus A350 cabin brackets — **30–55% weight reduction**)
> - **Medical:** Patient-specific titanium spinal cages and acetabular hip cups (biocompatible Ti-6Al-4V ELI)
> - **Automotive:** Conformal cooling channels in injection mold inserts (bismuth-tin, maraging steel)
> - **Energy:** Fuel cell and heat exchanger components with internal lattice structures

## 2.6 Defect Mechanisms — The "Trinity of SLM Defects"

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently ask about defect types. Memorize these three:

1. **Lack of Fusion (LoF) Porosity** — Insufficient energy → unmelted powder gaps. Caused by $E_v$ too low.
2. **Keyhole Porosity** — Excessive energy → vaporization of metal → entrapped gas bubbles. Caused by $E_v$ too high.
3. **Ballng Effect** — Plateau-Rayleigh instability of molten tracks → discontinuous tracks. Caused by poor wetting or excessive oxygen.

## 2.7 Microstructural Character (The "Why" of High Strength)

SLM parts exhibit:
- **Fine dendritic cells** (cell spacing 0.5–2 μm) due to rapid solidification
- **Anisotropic mechanical properties** (build direction vs. transverse)
- **High dislocation density** contributing to **yield strength 10–20% higher** than wrought counterparts
- **Residual tensile stresses** on top surface, **compressive** on bottom
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Volumetric Energy Density from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is **frequently asked in KTU Part B questions**. We start with the definition of energy per unit volume deposited by the laser.

### Given:
- Laser Power $P$ (W = J/s)
- Scan Speed $v$ (mm/s)
- Hatch Spacing $h$ (mm)
- Layer Thickness $t$ (mm)

### Step 1: Energy Delivered per Unit Time
The laser delivers energy at rate $P$ (in Watts, which equals J/s).

$$
\text{Energy per second} = P \quad [\text{J/s}]
$$

### Step 2: Energy Delivered per Unit Length of Scan
As the laser scans a single track, the time spent per unit length is:

$$
\text{Time per unit length} = \frac{1}{v} \quad [\text{s/mm}]
$$

Multiplying by power:

$$
\text{Energy per unit length} = \frac{P}{v} \quad [\text{J/mm}]
$$

### Step 3: Energy Delivered per Unit Area of a Single Track
If the laser scan track has effective width equal to the hatch spacing $h$, the energy per unit area is:

$$
\text{Energy per unit area} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h} \quad [\text{J/mm}^2]
$$

### Step 4: Energy Delivered per Unit Volume (VED)
Since each layer has thickness $t$, the energy per unit volume is:

$$
E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]
$$

**Result:** This is the canonical SLM energy density equation. The complete derivation in aligned format:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_v &= \frac{\text{Power}}{\text{Scan Speed} \times \text{Hatch Spacing} \times \text{Layer Thickness}} \\[6pt]
E_v &= \frac{P \, [\text{W}]}{v \, [\text{mm/s}] \cdot h \, [\text{mm}] \cdot t \, [\text{mm}]} \\[6pt]
E_v &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example (Worked)
**Problem:** An SLM machine processes Ti-6Al-4V with $P = 200$ W, $v = 800$ mm/s, $h = 0.1$ mm, and $t = 0.03$ mm. Calculate the volumetric energy density.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_v &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\[6pt]
E_v &= \frac{200 \text{ W}}{800 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.1 \text{ mm} \times 0.03 \text{ mm}} \\[6pt]
E_v &= \frac{200}{2.4} \\[6pt]
E_v &= 83.33 \text{ J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since the optimal $E_v$ for Ti-6Al-4V is **40–80 J/mm³**, this value (83.33) is **slightly high** — risk of keyholing and high residual stress. The student should recommend reducing $P$ to ~170 W or increasing $v$ to ~960 mm/s.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Build Time Estimation

### Given:
A part of volume $V$ (mm³) with average volumetric build rate $R_b = v \cdot h \cdot t$.

### Step 1: Build Rate Definition
Volume melted per second:

$$
R_b = v \cdot h \cdot t \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{s}]
$$

### Step 2: Build Time Formula

$$
T_{build} = \frac{V}{R_b} = \frac{V}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad [\text{s}]
$$

### Step 3: Conversion to Hours

$$
T_{build} \, [\text{hours}] = \frac{V}{3600 \cdot v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

### Numerical Example
A part has $V = 50{,}000$ mm³. With $v = 1000$ mm/s, $h = 0.12$ mm, $t = 0.03$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_b &= 1000 \times 0.12 \times 0.03 = 3.6 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} \\[6pt]
T_{build} &= \frac{50{,}000}{3.6} = 13{,}888.9 \text{ s} \\[6pt]
T_{build} &= \frac{13{,}888.9}{3600} \approx 3.86 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, build time is **2–5× longer** than this ideal estimate due to recoating time, gas purging, inter-layer dwell, and contour scanning passes. Add a **realistic overhead factor of 1.5–2×** for KTU calculations.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Density Optimization Window

The optimal energy density window can be derived by considering two boundary conditions:

### Lower Bound (Lack of Fusion Threshold)

$$
E_v^{min} = \frac{\rho \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0)}{A_{melt} / V_{track}}
$$

For 316L SS: $E_v^{min} \approx 55$ J/mm³

### Upper Bound (Keyhole Threshold)

$$
E_v^{max} \approx \frac{\pi \rho L_v^2}{4 (T_v - T_m)^2}
$$

For 316L SS: $E_v^{max} \approx 105$ J/mm³

### Optimal Processing Window

$$
E_v^{min} \leq E_v \leq E_v^{max}
$$

For 316L SS:

$$
55 \leq E_v \leq 105 \text{ J/mm}^3
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — SLM vs. Competing AM Processes

| Feature | **SLM (LPBF)** | EBM (Electron Beam) | DMLS (Direct Metal LS) | Binder Jetting |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Heat Source | **Yb-fiber laser** | Electron beam | Yb-fiber laser | Liquid binder |
| Atmosphere | **Inert gas (Ar/N₂)** | Vacuum | Inert gas | Air |
| Density | **> 99%** | > 99% | > 95% | ~ 60–80% (needs infiltration) |
| Materials | Steels, Ti, Al, Ni | Ti, Al (conductors) | Steels, bronze | Sand, steels, ceramics |
| Surface Finish | Ra ~ 10–20 μm | Ra ~ 25–40 μm | Ra ~ 15–25 μm | Ra ~ 5–10 μm |
| Residual Stress | High | Lower (preheated) | High | Low |
| Build Speed | Slow | **Fastest (high power)** | Slow | **Fastest (no melting)** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SLM System Architecture — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CAD Model Input STL File] --> B[Slicing Software Layer by Layer]
    B --> C[Build File G-Code / CLI]
    C --> D[Process Control Computer]
    D --> E[Laser System Yb Fiber 1070 nm]
    D --> F[Galvanometer Scanner XY Mirrors]
    D --> G[Build Platform Z Axis Motor]
    D --> H[Recoater Blade / Roller]
    D --> I[Heater Plate Temperature Control]
    D --> J[Inert Gas System Ar or N2 Supply]
    E --> K[Focusing Optics F-Theta Lens]
    F --> K
    K --> L[Laser Spot on Powder Bed]
    J --> M[Sealed Build Chamber O2 less than 100 ppm]
    L --> M
    I --> M
    H --> M
    G --> M
    M --> N[Recycled Powder Sieve and Reuse]
    M --> O[Finished Part on Build Plate]
```

## 4.2 SLM Process Flow — Layer-by-Layer Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Powder Recoating] --> P2[Step 2: Powder Bed Pre Heating]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Laser Scanning and Melting]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Solidification and Cooling]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Platform Lower by Delta Z]
    P5 --> Q{All Layers Complete?}
    Q -->|No| P1
    Q -->|Yes| P6[Step 6: Part Removal and Post Processing]
    P6 --> P7[Heat Treatment and HIP]
    P7 --> P8[Support Removal and Machining]
    P8 --> P9[Final Inspection CT Scanning]
```

## 4.3 Energy Density Decision Tree (Defect Diagnosis)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Calculate Volumetric Energy Density Ev] --> Q1{Ev within Optimal Window?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Good[Dense Part greater than 99 percent]
    Q1 -->|No Too Low| LoF[Defect: Lack of Fusion Porosity]
    Q1 -->|No Too High| KH[Defect: Keyhole Porosity]
    LoF --> Fix1[Increase P or Decrease v h or t]
    KH --> Fix2[Decrease P or Increase v h or t]
    Fix1 --> Start
    Fix2 --> Start
    Good --> End[Quality Part Achieved]
```

## 4.4 Schematic of Build Chamber Internals

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BuildChamber [Sealed Inert Atmosphere Chamber]
        direction TB
        subgraph TopModule [Laser Delivery Module]
            L[Laser Source Yb Fiber 1070 nm]
            SC[Galvanometer Scanner]
            FL[F-Theta Focusing Lens]
            L --> SC --> FL
        end
        subgraph MiddleModule [Powder Bed Module]
            RC[Recoater Blade]
            BP[Heated Build Platform]
            POWDER[Metal Powder Layer 20 to 100 micrometer]
            MP[Melt Pool Laser Spot]
            RC --> POWDER
            POWDER --> MP
            MP --> BP
        end
        subgraph BottomModule [Powder Supply Module]
            RSP[Powder Reservoir]
            RSP --> RC
        end
    end
    GAS[Inert Gas Inlet Argon or Nitrogen] --> BuildChamber
    EXH[Exhaust and Filtration] --> BuildChamber
    FL -.laser beam.-> MP
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**Q: Define Selective Laser Melting (SLM). Mention any two advantages of SLM over conventional manufacturing.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Selective Laser Melting (SLM) is a powder bed fusion additive manufacturing process in which a high-power laser (typically Yb-fiber, ~1070 nm) is used to **fully melt** metallic powder particles layer-by-layer in a **controlled inert atmosphere** to produce near-net-shape parts with **relative density > 99%** and mechanical properties comparable to wrought materials.

**Two Advantages over Conventional Manufacturing:**
1. **Geometric Complexity:** SLM can produce intricate internal features (lattice structures, conformal cooling channels) that are impossible or uneconomical to manufacture via CNC machining or casting.
2. **Material Efficiency:** Material utilization is **> 90%** (unsintered powder is recycled), compared to ~10–30% for traditional subtractive methods, drastically reducing raw material cost for expensive alloys like Ti-6Al-4V and Inconel 718.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**Q: Differentiate between Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) and Selective Laser Melting (SLM) in 4 points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | SLS | SLM |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Melting State** | Partial melting (sintering) | **Complete melting** above melting point |
| **Density Achieved** | 85–95% | **> 99%** |
| **Mechanical Properties** | Lower, anisotropic | **Comparable to wrought** |
| **Material Range** | Polymers + some metals | **Pure metals, alloys, composites** |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Explain the working principle of the Selective Laser Melting (SLM) process with a neat schematic. Discuss the role of the inert atmosphere. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define Volumetric Energy Density. For an SLM process with laser power 250 W, scan speed 1000 mm/s, hatch spacing 0.1 mm, and layer thickness 0.04 mm, calculate the energy density. Comment on whether this is suitable for processing Ti-6Al-4V. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Working Principle of SLM (7 marks):**

The SLM process operates on the **powder bed fusion** principle. The complete workflow is:

1. **CAD Preparation:** A 3D CAD model is converted to STL format and sliced into 2D cross-sections (~20–100 μm thick) using dedicated slicing software (e.g., Magics, Materialise, or OEM-specific tools).

2. **Powder Deposition:** A recoater blade or counter-rotating roller spreads a uniform layer of metal powder from the supply reservoir onto the **build platform** (substrate plate).

3. **Laser Melting:** A **Yb-fiber laser** (λ ≈ 1070 nm) is scanned across the powder bed via **galvanometer-driven mirrors** and focused by an **F-theta lens** to a spot size of 70–200 μm. The laser selectively melts the powder according to the current layer's geometry, fusing it to the previously solidified layer.

4. **Solidification:** The molten pool solidifies rapidly (**cooling rate 10⁴–10⁶ K/s**), forming metallurgical bonds. The build platform is maintained at an elevated temperature (80–200 °C) to reduce thermal gradients.

5. **Layer Step-Down:** The platform descends by one layer thickness (Δz), and the recoater deposits fresh powder. The cycle repeats until the part is complete.

6. **Post-Processing:** The part is removed from the build chamber, separated from the build plate, and subjected to **heat treatment, support removal, and surface finishing** (sandblasting, machining, or HIP).

**Role of the Inert Atmosphere (key points):**
- The build chamber is sealed and purged with **high-purity Argon (or Nitrogen)** to maintain **oxygen content below 1000 ppm** (often < 100 ppm for reactive metals like Ti).
- **Prevents oxidation** of the molten metal pool, which would otherwise form oxide inclusions that degrade mechanical properties.
- For **reactive metals (Ti, Al)**, even trace oxygen (O₂ > 500 ppm) causes embrittlement.
- Inert gas also **removes condensate and spatter** from the laser chamber via continuous flow.

> [Reference schematic: See SECTION 4.1 of these notes for functional block diagram]

**(b) Volumetric Energy Density Calculation (7 marks):**

**Definition:**
Volumetric Energy Density (VED) is the amount of laser energy delivered per unit volume of powder, mathematically expressed as:

$$
E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

**Given:**
- $P = 250$ W
- $v = 1000$ mm/s
- $h = 0.1$ mm
- $t = 0.04$ mm

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_v &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{250 \text{ W}}{1000 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.1 \text{ mm} \times 0.04 \text{ mm}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{250}{1000 \times 0.1 \times 0.04} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{250}{4} \\[6pt]
&= 62.5 \text{ J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Suitability for Ti-6Al-4V:**

The optimal energy density window for Ti-6Al-4V is **40–80 J/mm³** (per the KTU reference table).

- The calculated value of **62.5 J/mm³** lies within this window. ✅
- Therefore, this parameter set is **SUITABLE** for processing Ti-6Al-4V with expected density > 99% and minimal defects.
- However, the value is in the **mid-to-lower range**, meaning there is a slight risk of **lack-of-fusion porosity** if powder characteristics (flowability, size distribution) are not optimal. A small increase in laser power (~280 W) or decrease in scan speed would push the value into the safer central region (~70 J/mm³).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]

**Q: (a)** List and explain the four major process parameters of SLM. Describe how each affects part quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the three main types of defects encountered in SLM parts. Explain how the energy density window concept helps in defect avoidance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Four Major Process Parameters (7 marks):**

The four primary SLM process parameters are **laser power (P)**, **scan speed (v)**, **hatch spacing (h)**, and **layer thickness (t)**.

**1. Laser Power (P) [2 marks]:**
- **Definition:** Optical power output of the Yb-fiber laser, measured in Watts.
- **Range:** 100 W – 1 kW.
- **Effect on quality:** Higher power → deeper melt pool → better fusion with substrate layer. Too high → keyhole porosity, vaporization, balling. Too low → lack of fusion.

**2. Scan Speed (v) [2 marks]:**
- **Definition:** Linear velocity of the laser spot along the scan path.
- **Range:** 100 – 2000 mm/s.
- **Effect on quality:** Higher speed → less dwell time → less energy input → risk of lack-of-fusion. Lower speed → excessive energy → keyholing. Optimal speeds are material-dependent (e.g., ~800 mm/s for Ti-6Al-4V, ~600 mm/s for 316L).

**3. Hatch Spacing (h) [1.5 marks]:**
- **Definition:** Center-to-center distance between adjacent scan tracks.
- **Range:** 50 – 200 μm.
- **Effect on quality:** Smaller spacing → greater track overlap → denser parts but slower build. Larger spacing → risk of inter-track porosity if the melt pool width is less than the spacing.

**4. Layer Thickness (t) [1.5 marks]:**
- **Definition:** Vertical step (Δz) between successive layers.
- **Range:** 20 – 100 μm.
- **Effect on quality:** Thinner layers → finer resolution, smoother surface, better inter-layer bonding. Thicker layers → faster build but risk of delamination and poor fusion.

**(b) Three Main Defect Types in SLM (7 marks):**

**1. Lack-of-Fusion (LoF) Porosity [2 marks]:**
- **Cause:** Insufficient energy density ($E_v$ too low) due to low laser power, high scan speed, large hatch spacing, or thick layers.
- **Mechanism:** Powder particles are not fully melted, leaving **unmelted voids and inter-track gaps** between scan lines.
- **Appearance:** Irregular, sharp-edged pores (visible under optical microscopy).
- **Remedy:** Increase $P$, decrease $v$, $h$, or $t$.

**2. Keyhole Porosity [2 marks]:**
- **Cause:** Excessive energy density ($E_v$ too high).
- **Mechanism:** Local temperature exceeds the **boiling point** of the metal, causing vaporization. Vapor pressure depresses the melt pool surface, creating a deep **keyhole-shaped cavity** that may trap gas bubbles upon solidification.
- **Appearance:** Spherical pores (similar to gas porosity).
- **Remedy:** Decrease $P$, increase $v$, $h$, or $t$.

**3. Balling Phenomenon [1.5 marks]:**
- **Cause:** Plateau-Rayleigh instability of molten scan tracks, exacerbated by high surface tension, poor wetting, or high oxygen content.
- **Mechanism:** Continuous molten tracks break up into spherical balls due to surface tension minimization.
- **Remedy:** Optimize scan strategy, use preheating, ensure low oxygen environment, use lower surface tension alloys.

**Energy Density Window Concept [1.5 marks]:**
The energy density window defines the **range of $E_v$** between the **lower bound (lack-of-fusion threshold)** and the **upper bound (keyhole threshold)**. Operating within this window ensures:

$$
E_v^{LoF} \leq E_v \leq E_v^{Keyhole}
$$

This single equation encapsulates the interaction of all four process parameters and is the **primary quality control metric** in SLM process planning. KTU examiners use this as a standard grading checkpoint.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in SLM Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert layer thickness and hatch spacing from **micrometers (μm) to millimeters (mm)** before substituting into the energy density equation. This gives a result off by a factor of 1000 — instant zero.
>
> 2. **Confusing SLM with SLS:** Students frequently use "sintering" and "melting" interchangeably. SLM = **full melting** (density > 99%). SLS = **partial fusion** (density 85–95%). Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for this error.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Inert Gas:** In SLM process descriptions, ALWAYS mention the **inert atmosphere (Ar/N₂) and oxygen control (< 1000 ppm)**. Examiners specifically test this understanding.
>
> 4. **Energy Density Without Material Context:** Simply calculating $E_v$ is not enough. You must **comment on whether the value is suitable** by comparing it to the **material-specific optimal window**. Examiners expect this analysis.
>
> 5. **Wrong Process Name DMLS vs SLM:** DMLS (Direct Metal Laser Sintering, EOS term) and SLM are often used interchangeably in industry but differ technically. For KTU, use **SLM** as the generic academic term.
>
> 6. **Build Time Calculation Missing Overhead:** Always include a **realistic overhead factor (1.5–2×)** when calculating build time, accounting for recoating, gas purging, and contour scanning.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — SLM (Module 3)**

- [ ] **SLM = Powder Bed Fusion + Full Melting of Metal Powder** (vs. SLS = partial sintering)
- [ ] **Laser Type:** Yb-fiber, **wavelength ~1070 nm**, power 100 W – 1 kW
- [ ] **Atmosphere:** Inert gas (Ar or N₂), **O₂ < 1000 ppm**
- [ ] **Four Core Parameters:** $P$ (power), $v$ (scan speed), $h$ (hatch spacing), $t$ (layer thickness)
- [ ] **Master Equation (VED):** $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ **[J/mm³]**
- [ ] **Energy Density Windows (memorize):**
  - 316L SS: 60–100 J/mm³
  - Ti-6Al-4V: 40–80 J/mm³
  - AlSi10Mg: 30–70 J/mm³
  - Inconel 718: 50–90 J/mm³
- [ ] **Three Defect Types:** Lack-of-Fusion (low $E_v$), Keyhole (high $E_v$), Balling (oxidation/instability)
- [ ] **Build Rate:** $R_b = v \cdot h \cdot t$ [mm³/s]
- [ ] **Cooling Rate:** 10⁴ – 10⁶ K/s (rapid solidification → fine dendritic microstructure)
- [ ] **Density Achieved:** > 99% (near-wrought mechanical properties)
- [ ] **Key Applications:** Aerospace brackets, medical implants (Ti-6Al-4V), conformal cooling inserts
- [ ] **Build Platform Pre-heat:** 80–200 °C (reduces residual stress)
- [ ] **Powder Size:** Typically **15–45 μm** for SLM (finer than SLS)
- [ ] **Spot Size:** 70–200 μm
- [ ] **Recoating Method:** Blade or counter-rotating roller
- [ ] **Invention:** Fraunhofer ILT, Germany, 1995
- [ ] **Always** mention **inert atmosphere** and **oxygen control** in descriptive answers
- [ ] **Always** add a **realistic overhead factor (1.5–2×)** to build time calculations
- [ ] **Standard:** ISO/ASTM 52900 refers to SLM as **"Laser Powder Bed Fusion (LPBF)"** of metals
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 4D Printing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
