## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]**
*List any three applications of Additive Manufacturing in the biomedical field. For each, name the specific AM process most suited and justify in one line.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Patient-specific cranial implant:** SLM (PBF-LB-M) in Ti6Al4V — matches CT-derived geometry, allows porous surface for bone ingrowth.
2. **Surgical drill guide:** SLA (Vat Photopolymerization) in MED610 biocompatible resin — high accuracy (±0.05 mm), sterilizable, low cost per part.
3. **Tissue engineering scaffold:** Material Extrusion of hydrogel (GelMA/alginate) — permits controlled pore architecture for cell seeding and nutrient diffusion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write "AM is used in biomedical" without naming the **specific process**. Always pair the *application* with the *process* and the *material*. **3 marks lost = −3** for a vague answer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2023]**
*Explain with an example why Additive Manufacturing is preferred over conventional manufacturing in the aerospace industry.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
AM in aerospace is preferred because it (a) enables **topology optimization** for weight reduction, (b) **consolidates multi-part assemblies into single prints**, and (c) drastically reduces the **Buy-to-Fly ratio**.
*Example:* The **GE LEAP fuel nozzle** — originally assembled from 18 individual cast+welded nickel-superalloy parts, was redesigned as **one monolithic DMLM-printed nozzle**, achieving a **25 % weight reduction**, **5× durability**, and the elimination of brazed joints (a primary failure site).
*(Full-mark answer must include: process — DMLM; material — CoCr/Ni-superalloy; quantitative benefit — 25 % weight ↓, BTF drop from ~6:1 to 1:1.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

**Q3. (A) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024, Module 4]**
*With suitable case studies, discuss in detail the applications of Additive Manufacturing in the **biomedical** sector. Explain why AM has become a transformative technology for implants, surgical guides, and tissue engineering.*

**Model Answer (14 marks — split as Part a + Part b):**

### Part (a) — Patient-Specific Implants & Surgical Guides (7 marks)

**1. Cranial/Maxillofacial Implants (3 marks)**
- Workflow: **CT scan → DICOM → segmentation (Mimics/InVesalius) → STL → lattice design (nTopology/Magics) → SLM in Ti6Al4V ELI**.
- Case: **Oxford Performance Materials' PEEK cranial implant (FDA 2013)** — first 3D-printed implant to receive FDA clearance; printed in osteoconductive PEKK polymer.
- Why AM: **anatomical uniqueness** (no two skulls identical); conventional forging/CNC cannot match the geometry within acceptable lead time.

**2. Orthopedic (Hip, Knee, Spinal) — 4 marks**
- **Trabecular lattice structures** built using the **Gibson–Ashby** stiffness relation:
$$
E^* = C \cdot E_s \cdot \left(\frac{\rho^*}{\rho_s}\right)^n
$$
- For a **Ti6Al4V acetabular cup**, the lattice porosity (**60–80 %**) and pore size (**200–500 µm**) are tuned so that $E^*$ matches trabecular bone (≈ 0.5–15 GPa), avoiding *stress shielding* — a known cause of bone resorption in stiff metallic implants.
- Case: **Adler Ortho (Italy)** and **Lima Corporate (Italy)** — both commercially produce 3D-printed acetabular cups with trabecular titanium (TT).

### Part (b) — Surgical Guides, Dental & Tissue Engineering (7 marks)

**3. Surgical Guides & Dental (3 marks)**
- **Surgical drill guides:** SLA-printed in MED610 (Stratasys); guide the saw/jig in total knee arthroplasty with sub-millimeter accuracy.
- **Dental aligners:** Align Technology (Invisalign) — 3D-printed molds for thermoformed clear aligners; **>10 million** patients treated (as of 2023).
- **Dental crowns/bridges:** SLM in Co-Cr alloy, post-sintered, veneered with porcelain.

**4. Tissue Engineering & Bioprinting (4 marks)**
- **Scaffold-based approach:** PLGA/PCL scaffolds printed via Material Extrusion (FDM); then seeded with cells.
- **Bioprinting:** Multi-head extrusion of bioinks — cell-laden hydrogels (GelMA, alginate, collagen) layer-by-layer to mimic native ECM.
- **Case:** Organovo (USA) — first bioprinted liver tissue patches (ex-vivo testing 2014).
- Regulatory hurdle: full organ printing remains at research stage; cartilage and skin are closest to clinical use.

*(Conclude with: 3–4 lines on regulatory pathway — FDA 510(k), EU MDR 2017/745.)*

---

**Q3. (B) — ALTERNATIVE 14-Mark Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2023]**
*With suitable case studies, discuss the applications of Additive Manufacturing in the **aerospace and automotive** industries. Compare and contrast the engineering drivers and material choices in each sector.*

**Model Answer (14 marks — split as Part a + Part b):**

### Part (a) — Aerospace Applications (7 marks)

**1. Structural Brackets (2 marks)**
- Airbus A350 XWB **cabin brackets** in AlSi10Mg (SLM) — **30–50 % weight savings** vs. machined Al; **BTF ratio drop from ~15:1 to ~1:1**.
- Topology-optimized designs only feasible with AM.

**2. Rocket & Engine Components (3 marks)**
- **SpaceX SuperDraco** thruster chamber — DMLM-printed Inconel 718; first flight-certified AM rocket engine (**2014**).
- **GE LEAP fuel nozzle** — 18 parts → 1; CoCr superalloy, DMLM; **25 % weight ↓, 5× durability**.

**3. Satellite & Antenna (2 marks)**
- **Conformal RF antennas** on satellite bodies — topology + RF co-design (HFSS + nTopology) → single-piece AlSi10Mg with integrated waveguides.

**Driver:** **Weight reduction** is paramount — every 1 kg saved on a satellite ≈ **\$30,000** in launch cost; on a wide-body jet, ≈ **\$3,000/year/plane** in fuel.

### Part (b) — Automotive Applications (7 marks)

**1. End-Use Parts (3 marks)**
- **Bugatti Titanium Brake Caliper (2018)** — SLM in Ti6Al4V, world-record largest 3D-printed functional car component; 8.2 kg finished, **41 % lighter** than the Al counterpart, certified for **160+ km/h** operation.
- **Czinger 21C** — chassis printed via BAAM (Big Area Additive Manufacturing) in carbon-fiber-reinforced ABS; hybrid assembly.

**2. Prototyping & Tooling (2 marks)**
- **Functional concept models** in clay-like resins (PolyJet).
- **Sand casting cores** — Binder Jetted (Voxeljet, ExOne) for short-run prototype cylinder heads.

**3. EV & Future Mobility (2 marks)**
- **Lattice-based battery housings** with integrated cooling — weight + thermal management optimized simultaneously.
- **Customized interior trim** — mass-customization of trim panels (BMW iX Flow, Genesis).

**Comparison Table (must appear in answer):**

| Parameter | Aerospace | Automotive |
|---|---|---|
| Primary driver | **Weight, fuel efficiency, part consolidation** | **Volume production, lead time, customization** |
| Material | Ti alloys, Inconel, AlSi10Mg | Polymers, steel, Al, Ti (premium) |
| Process | DMLM (metal PBF) | FDM/PolyJet (prototypes), SLM (premium parts) |
| Build volume need | Small to medium | Large to extra-large (BAAM) |
| Cost per part tolerance | High (low volume) | Low (high volume) |
| Cert. | AS9100, FAA, EASA | IATF 16949, ISO/TS |

---

**Q4. (A) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024, Module 4]**
*Explain the emerging applications of Additive Manufacturing in **food and electronics** industries. Discuss the unique material, safety, and process constraints in each sector.*

**Model Answer Outline (14 marks — Part a + Part b):**

### Part (a) — Food Industry (7 marks)
- **Material constraints:** Only food-grade materials (FDA 21 CFR / EU 10/2011, FCM). Typical feedstocks: chocolate, sugar paste, dough, hummus, pureed vegetables, hydrocolloids.
- **Process constraints:** No high-temperature sintering (which is why metal PBF is *excluded*). **Paste extrusion** (syringe-based) and **binder jetting** (with edible binder, e.g., sugar–water on cocoa powder) dominate.
- **Case studies:**
  - **BeeHex** (NASA spin-off, 2014) — first 3D-printed pizza for astronaut nutrition.
  - **3D Systems ChefJet / Sugar Lab** — printed sugar sculptures for pastry.
  - **Natural Machines Foodini** — domestic food printer; layer-by-layer dough, sauce, cheese.
- **Application drivers:** *Personalized nutrition* (texture for dysphagia patients), *novel gastronomy* (multi-flavor and multi-texture single bite), *food waste reduction* (customized portion size).

### Part (b) — Electronics Industry (7 marks)
- **Material constraints:** Conductive inks (silver nanoparticles, copper, graphene), dielectric polymers, liquid metals (EGaIn — Gallium-Indium eutectic), photopolymers for dielectric layers.
- **Process constraints:** **Material jetting** (PolyJet, InkJet, Aerosol Jet) is preferred for mm-scale features (10 µm line width achievable with aerosol jet).
- **Case studies:**
  - **Nano Dimension DragonFly IV** — multi-material jetting of dielectric + silver ink for fully functional multilayer PCBs in <24 hrs.
  - **Voxel8** (USA) — first development kit for printing embedded electronics with conductive PLA + photopolymer.
  - **Optomec Aerosol Jet** — conformal 3D antennas on curved substrates (used in 5G, defense).
- **Application drivers:** *Rapid PCB prototyping*, *conformal/3D antennas*, *flexible hybrid electronics (FHE)*, *embedded sensors and IoT*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in 14-mark Questions:**
> 1. **Do not write only bullet points.** KTU 2024 expects descriptive, paragraph-style answers with at least **one case study per sub-part**.
> 2. **Quantify your answer.** Vague phrases like "weight is reduced" get **0 marks**; "25 % weight reduction in the GE LEAP fuel nozzle" gets **full credit**.
> 3. **Always name the AM process from the ISO/ASTM 52900 categories** (e.g., PBF-LB-M, DED-LB-M, VPP) — not just "3D printing".
> 4. **Include a comparison table or schematic** in Part (b) — it is worth 1–2 marks and demonstrates structured thinking.
> 5. **Avoid generic statements** like "AM is fast and cheap." *Always* state the metric (e.g., "lead time reduced by 70 %", "BTF from 20:1 to 1.5:1").

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Biomedical:** Three pillars — *Implants* (Ti6Al4V, PEEK, CoCr via SLM), *Surgical Guides* (SLA, MED610), *Bioprinting* (hydrogel extrusion). Key equation: **Gibson–Ashby** $E^* = C E_s (\rho^*/\rho_s)^n$.
- **Manufacturing:** *Conformal cooling* in mold inserts → cycle time −30 %. *Mass customization* (hearing aid industry 100 % AM).
- **Aerospace:** **GE LEAP** (DMLM, CoCr, 18→1 part), **SpaceX SuperDraco** (DMLM, Inconel, 1st flight-certified), **A350 brackets** (AlSi10Mg, 30–50 % weight ↓).
- **Automotive:** **Bugatti Ti caliper** (SLM, Ti6Al4V, 8.2 kg, 41 % weight ↓), **Local Motors Strata** (BAAM, 20 % CF-ABS, 44 h print), **Czinger 21C**.
- **Food:** Paste extrusion / binder jetting only; chocolate, dough, sugar; dysphagia, astronaut nutrition, gastronomy.
- **Electronics:** Material jetting (PolyJet, aerosol jet) of conductive + dielectric inks; **DragonFly IV** for PCBs; embedded liquid metal (EGaIn) sensors.
- **Universal Formulas to Memorize:**
$$
E_v = \frac{P}{v h t} \quad ; \quad BTF = \frac{m_{raw}}{m_{final}} \quad ; \quad W_{red} = 1 - \frac{m_{AM}}{m_{conv}}
$$
- **Standard Metrics (always quote):** resolution ±0.05–0.1 mm, Ra 5–20 µm (as-built), density >99.5 % (PBF), lead time ↓ 60–80 %, BTF 20:1 → 1:1.
- **Process Naming (ISO/ASTM 52900):** PBF-LB-M (SLM), PBF-LB-P (SLS), DED-LB-M, VPP (SLA/DLP), MEX (FDM/FFF), MJP (PolyJet), BJT (Binder Jetting).
- **Regulatory:** Biomedical → FDA 510(k), EU MDR 2017/745; Aerospace → AS9100, EASA/FAA; Food → FDA 21 CFR, EU 10/2011.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Last-Mile Advice:** For any application-sector question, structure your answer as **(1) Application areas → (2) Specific case study (named) → (3) Process + Material → (4) Quantitative benefit → (5) Limitations / Future scope.** This five-part structure is the exact blueprint of a 14-mark KTU model answer.
